topics.title: Adjustment Disorder And Acute Stress Reaction
topics.html:
Definition

Adjustment disorder is a maladaptive reaction to an identifiable stressor occurring within 3 months, with symptoms that do not meet criteria for another mental disorder and resolve within 6 months. Acute stress reaction (formerly acute stress disorder in DSM-IV) is a transient disturbance following trauma, lasting hours to days.

Core Clinical Content

Adjustment disorder criteria

Symptoms within 3 months of stressor; not meeting criteria for another disorder; resolves within 6 months

Adjustment disorder subtypes

Depressed mood, anxiety, mixed, disturbance of conduct, mixed disturbance

Adjustment disorder prevalence

Common; associated with life events (bereavement, divorce, illness, job loss)

Adjustment disorder treatment

Brief psychotherapy (CBT, supportive); usually self-limiting

Adjustment disorder vs bereavement

Bereavement: normal grief, not disorder; AD: symptoms disproportionate, impairs function

Adjustment disorder vs MDD

MDD: meets full criteria; AD: subsyndromal, tied to stressor

Acute stress reaction (ICD-11)

Transient; days; mixed anxiety-depression; dissociative symptoms

Acute stress disorder (DSM-5)

≥3 days to 1 month; trauma-related; similar to PTSD but shorter

Risk of PTSD

Acute stress disorder 50% progress to PTSD

Treatment

Brief CBT; watchful waiting; trauma-focused therapy if persistent

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TreatmentBrief CBT; watchful waiting; trauma-focused therapy if persistent

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Adjustment disorder and acute stress reaction?

Adjustment disorder is a maladaptive reaction to an identifiable stressor occurring within 3 months, with symptoms that do not meet criteria for another mental disorder and resolve within 6 months. Acute stress reaction (formerly acute stress disorder in DSM-IV) is a transient disturbance following trauma, lasting hours to days.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Adjustment disorder and acute stress reaction?

Key features include: adjustment disorder criteria, adjustment disorder subtypes, adjustment disorder prevalence.

Q3. How is Adjustment disorder and acute stress reaction diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Adjustment disorder and acute stress reaction?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Adjustment disorder and acute stress reaction?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria
  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

topics.title: Generalised Anxiety Disorder Gad
topics.html:
Definition

Generalised anxiety disorder (GAD) is characterised by excessive, uncontrollable worry about multiple events/activities for at least 6 months, accompanied by physical symptoms of anxiety (restlessness, fatigue, concentration difficulty, irritability, muscle tension, sleep disturbance).

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria

Excessive anxiety/worry ≥6 months, ≥3 of 6 associated symptoms

Associated symptoms

Restlessness, fatigue, concentration difficulty, irritability, muscle tension, sleep disturbance

Prevalence

Lifetime ~5-6%; 2x more common in women; median onset 30 years

Comorbidity

Depression (60%), other anxiety disorders, substance use

First-line treatment

SSRI/SNRI + CBT

Short-term anxiolytic

Benzodiazepine (max 2-4 weeks) for severe acute anxiety

Pregabalin

Alternative to benzodiazepine; reduced abuse potential

Buspirone

5-HT1A partial agonist; non-sedating, no dependence; takes 2-4 weeks

Cognitive behaviour therapy (CBT)

As effective as medication; lasting benefits

Course

Chronic, fluctuating; tends to improve with age

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
DSM-5 criteriaExcessive anxiety/worry ≥6 months, ≥3 of 6 associated symptoms
Associated symptomsRestlessness, fatigue, concentration difficulty, irritability, muscle tension, s

Buspirone5-HT1A partial agonist; non-sedating, no dependence; takes 2-4 weeks
Cognitive behaviour therapy (CBT)As effective as medication; lasting benefits
CourseChronic, fluctuating; tends to improve with age

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Generalised anxiety disorder (GAD)?

Generalised anxiety disorder (GAD) is characterised by excessive, uncontrollable worry about multiple events/activities for at least 6 months, accompanied by physical symptoms of anxiety (restlessness, fatigue, concentration difficulty, irritability, muscle tension, sleep disturbance).

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Generalised anxiety disorder (GAD)?

Key features include: dsm-5 criteria, associated symptoms, prevalence. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Generalised anxiety disorder (GAD) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Generalised anxiety disorder (GAD)?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Generalised anxiety disorder (GAD)?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Generalised anxiety disorder (GAD) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

topics.title: Hoarding Disorder And Body Dysmorphic Disorder
topics.html:
Definition

Hoarding disorder is characterised by persistent difficulty discarding or parting with possessions regardless of value, due to perceived need to save and distress associated with discarding. Body dysmorphic disorder (BDD) is preoccupation with perceived defects in physical appearance that are not observable or appear slight, causing significant distress.

Core Clinical Content

Hoarding disorder DSM-5

Persistent difficulty discarding possessions; cluttered living space; distress/impairment

Hoarding prevalence

~2-6%; increases with age; more common in older adults

Hoarding vs OCD

Hoarding: ego-syntonic, no obsessions; OCD: ego-dystonic, obsessions present

Hoarding vs collecting

Collectors: organised, valued, displayed; hoarders: cluttered, distress, impairment

Hoarding treatment

CBT (most evidence-based); decluttering; motivational interviewing; SSRIs sometimes

BDD definition

Preoccupation with perceived physical defects not visible to others; significant distress

BDD prevalence

~2%; often begins in adolescence; equal M:F

BDD common concerns

Skin, hair, nose, body shape, symmetry; mirror checking, camouflaging

BDD comorbidity

Depression (60-80%), OCD (30%), social anxiety, eating disorders

BDD treatment

SSRI (fluoxetine, fluvoxamine); CBT (ERP + cognitive restructuring); clomipramine

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Q1. What is the definition of Hoarding disorder and body dysmorphic disorder?

Hoarding disorder is characterised by persistent difficulty discarding or parting with possessions regardless of value, due to perceived need to save and distress associated with discarding. Body dysmorphic disorder (BDD) is preoccupation with perceived defects in physical appearance that are not observable or appear slight, causing significant distress.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Hoarding disorder and body dysmorphic disorder?

Key features include: hoarding disorder dsm-5, hoarding prevalence, hoarding vs ocd.

Q3. How is Hoarding disorder and body dysmorphic disorder diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Hoarding disorder and body dysmorphic disorder?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Hoarding disorder and body dysmorphic disorder?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

topics.title: Obsessive Compulsive Disorder Ocd
topics.html:
Definition

Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) is characterised by obsessions (recurrent, intrusive, distressing thoughts/images) and/or compulsions (repetitive behaviours or mental acts performed to reduce anxiety), causing significant impairment.

Core Clinical Content

Obsessions

Recurrent, intrusive, unwanted thoughts/images; recognised as own (ego-dystonic)

Compulsions

Repetitive behaviours or mental acts; aimed at reducing anxiety

Common obsessions

Contamination, harm, symmetry, sexual, religious, somatic

Common compulsions

Washing, checking, counting, ordering, repeating, mental rituals

Insight

Usually preserved (ego-dystonic)

Prevalence

Lifetime ~2-3%; equal M:F; onset usually in adolescence/young adulthood

Comorbidity

Depression (60%), tic disorders, anxiety, OCPD (different condition)

First-line treatment

SSRI (higher dose than for depression) + CBT (ERP — exposure and response prevention)

SSRI dose

Often need higher doses (e.g., fluoxetine 60-80 mg) for OCD

Onset of action

Slower than for depression (8-12 weeks for full effect)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

CompulsionsRepetitive behaviours or mental acts; aimed at reducing anxiety

First-line treatmentSSRI (higher dose than for depression) + CBT (ERP — exposure and response preven
SSRI doseOften need higher doses (e.g., fluoxetine 60-80 mg) for OCD
Onset of actionSlower than for depression (8-12 weeks for full effect)

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD)?

Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) is characterised by obsessions (recurrent, intrusive, distressing thoughts/images) and/or compulsions (repetitive behaviours or mental acts performed to reduce anxiety), causing significant impairment.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD)?

Key features include: obsessions, compulsions, common obsessions. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD)?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD)?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

topics.title: Panic Disorder And Panic Attacks
topics.html:
Definition

Panic disorder is characterised by recurrent, unexpected panic attacks (discrete episodes of intense fear with physical/cognitive symptoms) followed by persistent concern about further attacks, leading to behavioural change (agoraphobia).

Core Clinical Content

Panic attack

Discrete period of intense fear, ≥4 of 13 symptoms, peaks within minutes

Panic attack symptoms

Palpitations, sweating, trembling, dyspnoea, choking, chest pain, nausea, dizziness, derealisation, paraesthesia, chills, fear of dying/losing control

Duration

Typically 5-30 min, peaks within 10 min

Panic disorder

Recurrent unexpected attacks + persistent concern (≥1 month)

Agoraphobia

Fear of places where escape might be difficult; often develops with panic

Differential

MI, PE, hyperthyroidism, arrhythmias, phaeochromocytoma

First-line treatment

SSRI + CBT (exposure therapy)

Acute treatment

Benzodiazepine (short-acting) for acute attack

Hyperventilation

Often present; breathing exercises can help

Prognosis

Chronic, fluctuating; good response to treatment; 50-70% recover with treatment

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Phobias Specific Social Agoraphobia
topics.html:
Definition

Phobias are anxiety disorders characterised by excessive, persistent, irrational fear of a specific object, situation, or activity, leading to avoidance and significant functional impairment.

Core Clinical Content

Specific phobia

Fear of specific object/animal/situation (spiders, heights, blood, flying)

Types of specific phobia

Animal, natural environment, blood-injection-injury, situational, other

Social anxiety disorder

Fear of social situations where scrutiny is possible; performance vs generalised

Agoraphobia

Fear of ≥2 of: public transport, open spaces, enclosed places, queues, crowds, going out alone

Prevalence

Specific phobia 7-9%, social anxiety 7%, agoraphobia 1.7%

Treatment of specific phobia

topics.title: Post Traumatic Stress Disorder Ptsd
topics.html:
Definition

Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) develops after exposure to a traumatic event, characterised by re-experiencing, avoidance, negative cognitions/mood, and hyperarousal, lasting >1 month.

Core Clinical Content

Trauma criteria

Exposure to actual/threatened death, serious injury, or sexual violence

DSM-5 symptom clusters

Intrusion (re-experiencing), Avoidance, Negative alterations in cognition/mood, Hyperarousal

Duration

≥1 month (if <3 months = acute; >3 months = chronic)

Complex PTSD

PTSD + affect dysregulation, negative self-concept, relationship difficulties

Risk factors

Severity of trauma, prior trauma, lack of support, female sex

First-line trauma-focused therapy

TF-CBT, EMDR (eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing)

First-line medication

SSRI (sertraline, paroxetine, fluoxetine) — FDA approved

Prazosin

For trauma-related nightmares (off-label)

Acute stress disorder

Lasts 3 days to 1 month; same symptoms as PTSD; if >1 month → PTSD

Adjustment disorder

Milder, more common, develops within 3 months of stressor

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Capacity Assessment
topics.html:
Definition

Capacity (or competence) is the ability to understand, retain, weigh, and communicate a decision about a specific medical intervention at a particular time. It is decision-specific and time-specific.

Core Clinical Content

Four criteria of capacity (UK MCA 2005)

Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

Capacity is decision-specific

Can have capacity for one decision but not another

Capacity is time-specific

Can fluctuate; review when condition changes

Presumption of capacity

Adults presumed to have capacity unless assessed otherwise

Who assesses capacity?

Treating clinician (not a psychiatrist necessarily)

Two-stage test

(1) Diagnostic — does the person have impairment of mind/brain? (2) Functional — does the impairment affect capacity for this decision?

Common scenarios

Refusal of treatment, advance decisions, research participation, financial decisions

If lacks capacity

Best interests decision, advance decision, IMCA, court of protection

Communication aids

Use interpreters, sign language, simple language, visual aids

Adults with capacity can make unwise decisions

Capacity ≠ 'right' decision

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Mental State Examination Mse
topics.html:
Definition

The mental state examination (MSE) is a structured assessment of a patient's current mental state, organised by Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought, Perception, Cognition, Insight, and Risk.

Core Clinical Content

MSE components

Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form/content, Perception, Cognition, Insight, Risk

Appearance

Dress, grooming, hygiene, apparent age, distinguishing features

Behaviour

Eye contact, rapport, psychomotor activity (retardation, agitation), abnormal movements

Speech

Rate, rhythm, volume, quantity, spontaneity, latency, abnormalities (pressure, mute)

Mood

Subjective emotional state (euthymic, depressed, elevated, anxious, irritable)

Affect

Observed emotional expression (broad, restricted, blunted, flat, labile, congruent)

Thought form

Linear, circumstantial, tangential, flight of ideas, loosening of associations, neologisms

Thought content

Preoccupations, obsessions, phobias, delusions, suicidal/homicidal ideation

Perception

Illusions, hallucinations (auditory, visual, olfactory, gustatory, tactile)

Cognition

Orientation, attention, concentration, memory (immediate, recent, remote)

Insight

Patient's understanding of their condition; levels 0-3 (none, partial, full)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Mental state examination (MSE)?

The mental state examination (MSE) is a structured assessment of a patient's current mental state, organised by Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought, Perception, Cognition, Insight, and Risk.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Mental state examination (MSE)?

Key features include: mse components, appearance, behaviour. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Mental state examination (MSE) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Mental state examination (MSE)?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Mental state examination (MSE)?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Mental state examination (MSE) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

topics.title: Psychiatric History Taking
topics.html:
Definition

Psychiatric history taking is a comprehensive assessment of the patient's presenting complaint, history of present illness, past psychiatric and medical history, medication, family history, personal/social history, premorbid personality, and substance use.

Core Clinical Content

Presenting complaint (PC)

Patient's own words for why they are seeking help

History of present illness (HPI)

Chronological narrative of current problem

Past psychiatric history

Previous episodes, diagnoses, treatments, hospitalisations

Past medical history

Medical conditions, especially relevant (e.g., thyroid, neurological)

Medication history

Current and past medications; OTC, supplements, recreational

Family history

Psychiatric disorders, suicide, substance use in first-degree relatives

Personal history

Childhood, education, occupation, relationships, living situation

Premorbid personality

Patient's personality before the illness

Substance use history

Tobacco, alcohol, illicit drugs — type, amount, duration, route

Forensic history

Criminal record, violence, legal issues

Sexual history

When relevant (relationship, orientation, STIs, dysfunction)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Risk Assessment In Psychiatry Suicide Violence Sel
topics.html:
Definition

Psychiatric risk assessment is the systematic evaluation of a patient's risk of suicide, self-harm, violence to others, self-neglect, and vulnerability, used to guide management decisions including hospitalisation and supervision.

Core Clinical Content

Suicide risk factors

Previous attempt, mental illness (depression, schizophrenia), substance use, social isolation, chronic pain, terminal illness, access to means

Suicide protective factors

Social support, family, reasons for living, religious beliefs, treatment engagement

SAD PERSONS scale

Sex, Age, Depression, Previous attempt, Ethanol, Rationality loss, Social support lacking, Organised plan, No spouse, Sickness

High-risk features

Active suicidal ideation with plan and means, prior attempt, intent

Ask directly about suicide

Does NOT increase risk; can decrease; always ask

Violence risk factors

Past violence, substance use, paranoid ideation, command hallucinations, personality disorder

HCR-20

Historical, Clinical, Risk Management violence risk assessment tool

Self-harm

Distinguish from suicide attempt; often relieves tension, no intent to die

Risk management

Remove means, supervised care, hospitalisation if high risk, crisis plan

Documentation

Document risk assessment, plan, discussion with patient and family

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Suicide risk factorsPrevious attempt, mental illness (depression, schizophrenia), substance use, soc
Suicide protective factorsSocial support, family, reasons for living, religious beliefs, treatment engagem
SAD PERSONS scaleSex, Age, Depression, Previous attempt, Ethanol, Rationality loss, Social suppor
High-risk featuresActive suicidal ideation with plan and means, prior attempt, intent

Risk managementRemove means, supervised care, hospitalisation if high risk, crisis plan
DocumentationDocument risk assessment, plan, discussion with patient and family

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm)?

Psychiatric risk assessment is the systematic evaluation of a patient's risk of suicide, self-harm, violence to others, self-neglect, and vulnerability, used to guide management decisions including hospitalisation and supervision.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm)?

Key features include: suicide risk factors, suicide protective factors, sad persons scale. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm)?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Risk assessment in psychiatry (suicide, violence, self-harm)?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

topics.title: The Doctor Patient Relationship And Communication
topics.html:
Definition

The doctor-patient relationship is the foundation of medical psychiatry, encompassing the professional bond, communication, and trust that enables therapeutic engagement. Effective communication in psychiatric assessment includes active listening, empathy, attending to nonverbal cues, and managing the emotional content of the interview.

Core Clinical Content

Calgary-Cambridge model

Framework for medical communication: initiating, gathering info, providing structure, building relationship, explaining, closing

Patient-centred interview

Focus on patient's perspective, ideas, concerns, expectations (ICE)

Open vs closed questions

Open for narrative; closed for clarification

Active listening

Verbal (reflection, paraphrasing) + nonverbal (eye contact, posture)

Empathy

Ability to understand patient's feelings; core therapeutic skill

Rapport

Foundation for trust and disclosure

Silence

Allows patient to reflect and elaborate

Transference

Patient's feelings about clinician (from past relationships)

Countertransference

Clinician's emotional response to patient

Breaking bad news

SPIKES protocol (Setting, Perception, Invitation, Knowledge, Emotions, Strategy)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Acute Behavioural Disturbance And Rapid Tranquilis
topics.html:
Definition

Acute behavioural disturbance is a clinical state of severe agitation, aggression, or violence posing immediate risk to the patient or others, often requiring rapid tranquilisation with antipsychotics and/or benzodiazepines after verbal de-escalation fails.

Core Clinical Content

Stepwise management

Verbal de-escalation → oral medication → IM medication → physical restraint (last resort)

Verbal de-escalation

Calm tone, clear language, offer choices, time, place of safety

Rapid tranquilisation (RT)

Use of medication to rapidly calm agitated patient; lowest effective dose

RT medications

IM lorazepam + haloperidol; olanzapine IM; aripiprazole IM; midazolam IM

Avoid benzodiazepines in

Respiratory depression risk; substance use, frailty

Avoid haloperidol in

Parkinsonism, Lewy body dementia (severe sensitivity), prolonged QT

Antipsychotic + benzodiazepine

Often combined; haloperidol 5 mg + lorazepam 2 mg IM is common RT combination

Dosing interval

Repeat every 30-60 min as needed; do not exceed BNF max

Monitoring post-RT

BP, pulse, SpO2, temperature, GCS, hydration; ECG if possible

Physical restraint

Last resort; rapid tranquilisation preferred; face-to-face review within 1 h

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Acute behavioural disturbance and rapid tranquilisation?

Key features include: stepwise management, verbal de-escalation, rapid tranquilisation (rt).

Q3. How is Acute behavioural disturbance and rapid tranquilisation diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Acute behavioural disturbance and rapid tranquilisation?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Acute behavioural disturbance and rapid tranquilisation?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

topics.title: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome Nms
topics.html:
Definition

Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) is a life-threatening idiosyncratic reaction to antipsychotics (dopamine antagonists) characterised by hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, autonomic instability, and altered mental status.

Core Clinical Content

NMS tetrad

Hyperthermia, muscle rigidity ('lead pipe'), autonomic instability, altered consciousness

Onset

Hours to days after antipsychotic change (rapid dose increase, parenteral)

Risk factors

High-potency typical antipsychotics, rapid dose escalation, dehydration, NMS history

Investigations

↑ CK (often >1000), ↑ WCC, ↓ Na, ↑ K (rhabdomyolysis), abnormal LFTs, myoglobinuria

Differential

Serotonin syndrome, malignant hyperthermia, anticholinergic toxicity, catatonia

Serotonin vs NMS

Serotonin: hyperreflexia, clonus, on serotonergic drugs; NMS: rigidity, bradyreflexia, on antipsychotics

Treatment

STOP antipsychotic; supportive care (ICU); dantrolene, bromocriptine, ECT in severe

Dantrolene

Muscle relaxant; reduces hyperthermia/rigidity

Bromocriptine

Dopamine agonist; reverses NMS pathophysiology

Prognosis

5-20% mortality if untreated; 80% recover with prompt recognition and treatment

topics.title: Serotonin Syndrome And Catatonia
topics.html:
Definition

Serotonin syndrome is a potentially life-threatening condition caused by excess serotonergic activity, characterised by cognitive, autonomic, and somatic features. Catatonia is a neuropsychiatric syndrome of psychomotor dysregulation with multiple subtypes.

Core Clinical Content

Serotonin syndrome triad

Cognitive (agitation, confusion), autonomic (fever, tachycardia, hypertension), somatic (clonus, hyperreflexia, tremor)

Cause

Serotonergic drugs (SSRIs + MAOIs, tramadol, linezolid, St John's Wort, triptans)

Onset

Within hours of serotonergic drug change

Clonus

Inducible, spontaneous, ocular — hallmark of serotonin syndrome

Treatment

STOP serotonergic drugs; supportive care; cyproheptadine (5-HT2A antagonist)

Cyproheptadine

5-HT2A antagonist; for moderate-severe cases

Catatonia features

Mutism, stupor, negativism, waxy flexibility, echolalia, echopraxia, posturing

Catatonia subtypes

Retarded (withdrawn), excited, malignant (with autonomic instability)

Catatonia causes

Mood disorders (esp. bipolar), schizophrenia, medical, drug-induced

Lorazepam challenge

1-2 mg IV/IM lorazepam; rapid response confirms catatonia; ECT for refractory

topics.title: Cognitive Behavioural Therapy Cbt And Related Ther
topics.html:
Definition

Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) is a structured, time-limited, present-focused psychotherapy that identifies and modifies unhelpful thinking patterns (cognitions) and behaviours to improve emotional wellbeing. Related therapies include DBT, ACT, and mindfulness-based CBT.

Core Clinical Content

CBT principles

Cognitive model (thoughts → feelings → behaviour); collaborative empiricism; Socratic questioning

CBT techniques

Thought records, behavioural activation, exposure, cognitive restructuring, problem-solving

CBT duration

Usually 12-20 sessions; structured; homework between sessions

CBT effectiveness

Strong evidence for depression, anxiety, OCD, PTSD, eating disorders

Cognitive distortions

Catastrophising, all-or-nothing thinking, mind-reading, overgeneralisation, personalisation

Behavioural activation

Schedule activities; counteracts withdrawal in depression

Exposure therapy

Graded exposure to feared stimuli; for phobias, OCD, PTSD

Dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT)

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and related therapies?

Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) is a structured, time-limited, present-focused psychotherapy that identifies and modifies unhelpful thinking patterns (cognitions) and behaviours to improve emotional wellbeing. Related therapies include DBT, ACT, and mindfulness-based CBT.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and related therapies?

Key features include: cbt principles, cbt techniques, cbt duration.

Q3. How is Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and related therapies diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and related therapies?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) and related therapies?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

topics.title: Other Therapies Dbt Emdr Mindfulness
topics.html:
Definition

Other evidence-based psychotherapies include dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT) for BPD, eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing (EMDR) for PTSD, mindfulness-based therapies, and motivational interviewing for substance use.

Core Clinical Content

DBT for BPD

Individual + group therapy + phone coaching; 4 modules: mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, interpersonal effectiveness

DBT duration

1-3 years; weekly individual + group sessions

DBT key skills

DEAR MAN (interpersonal), TIPP (distress tolerance), PLEASE (emotion regulation)

EMDR

Eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing; for PTSD; 8 phases

EMDR mechanism

Bilateral stimulation (eye movements) while recalling traumatic memory; debated

Mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR)

8-week program; for chronic stress, anxiety, depression relapse

Motivational interviewing (MI)

For substance use; collaborative, evocative, autonomy-supporting

MI principles

Express empathy, develop discrepancy, roll with resistance, support self-efficacy

Exposure therapy for PTSD

Prolonged exposure (PE), cognitive processing therapy (CPT)

EMDR vs exposure

EMDR includes bilateral stimulation; PE is pure exposure; both effective for PTSD

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Psychodynamic And Other Psychotherapies
topics.html:
Definition

Psychodynamic psychotherapy explores unconscious conflicts, early life experiences, and the therapeutic relationship to promote insight and change. Other psychotherapies include interpersonal therapy (IPT), family therapy, and group therapy.

Core Clinical Content

Psychodynamic therapy

Explores unconscious conflicts, transference, defence mechanisms; longer-term

Defence mechanisms

Denial, projection, displacement, sublimation, rationalisation, reaction formation, repression

Transference

Patient's feelings about therapist (from past relationships)

Countertransference

Therapist's emotional response to patient

Psychodynamic therapy uses

Personality disorders, chronic depression, anxiety, eating disorders

Interpersonal therapy (IPT)

Time-limited (12-16 sessions); focuses on interpersonal problems; for depression

IPT focus areas

Grief, role disputes, role transitions, interpersonal deficits

Family therapy

Treats family as a system; for schizophrenia, eating disorders, family conflict

Group therapy

Therapeutic factors: universality, altruism, instillation of hope, catharsis

Supportive psychotherapy

Active listening, empathy, reassurance; for chronic conditions, crisis

Q: Transference?

A: Patient's feelings about therapist (from past relationships)

Q: Countertransference?

A: Therapist's emotional response to patient

Q: Psychodynamic therapy uses?

A: Personality disorders, chronic depression, anxiety, eating disorders

Q: Interpersonal therapy (IPT)?

A: Time-limited (12-16 sessions); focuses on interpersonal problems; for depression

Q: IPT focus areas?

A: Grief, role disputes, role transitions, interpersonal deficits

Q: Family therapy?

A: Treats family as a system; for schizophrenia, eating disorders, family conflict

topics.title: Antidepressants Ssri Snri Tca Maoi Atypicals
topics.html:
Definition

Antidepressants are medications used to treat major depressive disorder and other psychiatric conditions. They work by modulating monoamine neurotransmitters (serotonin, noradrenaline, dopamine) and include SSRIs, SNRIs, TCAs, MAOIs, and atypical agents.

Core Clinical Content

SSRI mechanism

Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor; ↑ serotonin in synapse

Common SSRIs

Fluoxetine, sertraline, citalopram, escitalopram, paroxetine

SSRI side effects

GI upset, sexual dysfunction, hyponatraemia (SIADH), bleeding risk, QTc (citalopram)

SSRI discontinuation

Discontinuation syndrome (dizziness, nausea, paresthesia) — taper slowly

SNRI

Duloxetine, venlafaxine; serotonin + noradrenaline reuptake inhibition

SNRI uses

Depression, GAD, neuropathic pain (duloxetine), fibromyalgia

TCA mechanism

Serotonin + noradrenaline reuptake inhibition (also anticholinergic, antihistamine)

TCA side effects

Anticholinergic (dry mouth, urinary retention), sedation, orthostatic hypotension, QTc prolongation, fatal in overdose

TCA overdose

Cardiotoxic (VT, torsades), seizures, anticholinergic; sodium bicarbonate for VT

MAOI

Phenelzine, tranylcypromine; dietary restrictions (tyramine → hypertensive crisis); rarely used

Antidepressants are medications used to treat major depressive disorder and other psychiatric conditions. They work by modulating monoamine neurotransmitters (serotonin, noradrenaline, dopamine) and include SSRIs, SNRIs, TCAs, MAOIs, and atypical agents.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Antidepressants (SSRI, SNRI, TCA, MAOI, atypicals)?

Key features include: ssri mechanism, common ssris, ssri side effects.

Q3. How is Antidepressants (SSRI, SNRI, TCA, MAOI, atypicals) diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Antidepressants (SSRI, SNRI, TCA, MAOI, atypicals)?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Antidepressants (SSRI, SNRI, TCA, MAOI, atypicals)?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

topics.title: Antipsychotic Induced Movement Disorders Eps And A
topics.html:
Definition

Antipsychotic-induced movement disorders include extrapyramidal symptoms (acute dystonia, akathisia, parkinsonism, tardive dyskinesia) and related conditions. Anticholinergic toxicity is a serious adverse effect of drugs with anticholinergic properties (TCAs, antipsychotics), characterised by classic antimuscarinic features.

Core Clinical Content

EPS types

Acute dystonia, Akathisia, Parkinsonism, Tardive dyskinesia (DAPT)

Acute dystonia

Hours-days; sustained muscle contraction (torticollis, oculogyric crisis); treat with anticholinergic (procyclidine IM)

Akathisia

Days-weeks; subjective restlessness; treat with β-blocker (propranolol), benzodiazepine, dose reduction

Drug-induced parkinsonism

Weeks-months; bradykinesia, rigidity, tremor; treat with anticholinergic; switch to atypical

Tardive dyskinesia (TD)

Months-years; orofacial dyskinesia, choreoathetoid movements; often IRREVERSIBLE

TD risk factors

Older age, female, longer duration, higher dose, typical antipsychotics, mood disorder

TD treatment

Stop/reduce antipsychotic; switch to clozapine; VMAT2 inhibitors (valbenazine, deutetrabenazine)

Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS)

Hyperthermia, rigidity, autonomic instability, altered consciousness; emergency

Anticholinergic toxicity — central

Confusion, agitation, hallucinations, hyperthermia, seizures

Anticholinergic toxicity — peripheral

Dry mouth, blurred vision, urinary retention, constipation, mydriasis, tachycardia, flushed skin

Anticholinergic toxicity is a serious adverse effect of drugs with anticholinergic properties (TCAs, antipsychotics), characterised by classic antimuscarinic features.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Antipsychotic-induced movement disorders (EPS) and anticholinergic toxicity?

topics.title: Antipsychotics Typical Atypical And Clozapine
topics.html:
Definition

Antipsychotics are dopamine D2 receptor antagonists used to treat psychotic disorders. They are classified as typical (first-generation) or atypical (second-generation) based on side effect profiles and serotonin/dopamine receptor activity.

Core Clinical Content

Typical (first-gen)

Haloperidol, chlorpromazine, fluphenazine, zuclopenthixol; higher EPS risk

Atypical (second-gen)

Risperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine, aripiprazole, clozapine; lower EPS risk

Dopamine pathways

Mesolimbic (positive symptoms), mesocortical (negative), nigrostriatal (EPS), tuberoinfundibular (prolactin)

Mechanism of antipsychotics

D2 receptor antagonism; atypicals also block 5-HT2A

Extrapyramidal side effects (EPS)

Acute dystonia, akathisia, parkinsonism, tardive dyskinesia

Metabolic side effects (atypicals)

Weight gain, dyslipidaemia, diabetes (especially olanzapine, clozapine)

Hyperprolactinaemia

Risperidone, amisulpride, paliperidone

QTc prolongation

Ziprasidone, sertindole; caution in cardiac disease

Clozapine — reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia

Requires regular FBC (agranulocytosis risk); register monitoring

Clozapine side effects

Agranulocytosis, myocarditis, weight gain, diabetes, sedation, sialorrhoea, seizures

Antipsychotics are dopamine D2 receptor antagonists used to treat psychotic disorders. They are classified as typical (first-generation) or atypical (second-generation) based on side effect profiles and serotonin/dopamine receptor activity.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Antipsychotics (typical-atypical) and clozapine?

Key features include: typical (first-gen), atypical (second-gen), dopamine pathways.

Q3. How is Antipsychotics (typical-atypical) and clozapine diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Antipsychotics (typical-atypical) and clozapine?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Antipsychotics (typical-atypical) and clozapine?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

topics.title: Anxiolytics And Hypnotics Benzodiazepines Z Drugs
topics.html:
Definition

Anxiolytics and hypnotics are medications used to treat anxiety and insomnia. The main classes are benzodiazepines, Z-drugs (non-benzodiazepine hypnotics), buspirone, and beta-blockers (for performance anxiety).

Core Clinical Content

Benzodiazepine mechanism

↑ GABA-A receptor activity (positive allosteric modulator)

Benzodiazepine examples

Diazepam (long-acting), lorazepam (intermediate), temazepam (short-acting)

Benzodiazepine uses

Anxiety, insomnia, alcohol withdrawal, status epilepticus, procedural sedation

Benzodiazepine side effects

Sedation, dependence, tolerance, falls, respiratory depression (with opioids)

Benzodiazepine dependence

Develops within weeks; withdrawal can be life-threatening (seizures)

Benzodiazepine withdrawal

Taper slowly (5-10% every 1-2 weeks); convert to long-acting (diazepam) first

Z-drugs

Zolpidem, zopiclone, zaleplon; non-benzodiazepine hypnotics; same risks

Buspirone

5-HT1A partial agonist; anxiolytic; non-sedating; no dependence; takes 2-4 weeks

Pregabalin

α2δ ligand; reduces glutamate; anxiolytic (GAD), neuropathic pain, epilepsy

Beta-blockers (propranolol)

For performance anxiety (blocks peripheral symptoms); not for GAD

Definition

Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) is a safe and highly effective treatment for severe psychiatric conditions, especially treatment-resistant depression, with electrical current inducing a generalised seizure under anaesthesia. Other biological treatments include TMS, ketamine, and esketamine.

Core Clinical Content

ECT mechanism

Induced generalised seizure under anaesthesia + muscle relaxant

ECT indications

Severe/treatment-resistant depression, catatonia, psychotic depression, NMS, severe mania, pregnancy (preferred)

ECT efficacy

60-80% response in severe depression; rapid effect (1-2 weeks)

ECT course

Usually 6-12 sessions; 2-3 per week

ECT side effects

Memory impairment (retrograde/anterograde), headache, nausea, anaesthesia risks

ECT contraindications

No absolute contraindications; relative: ↑ ICP, recent MI/stroke, pregnancy (modified ECT safe)

ECT in pregnancy

Safe; preferred for severe depression; avoid hyperventilation; protect airway

Transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS)

Non-invasive; for treatment-resistant depression; rTMS effective

Esketamine (Spravato)

NMDA antagonist; intranasal; for treatment-resistant depression (with SSRI)

Ketamine

IV; rapid antidepressant effect (hours); potential abuse; research/limited use

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of ECT (electroconvulsive therapy) and other biological treatments?

Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) is a safe and highly effective treatment for severe psychiatric conditions, especially treatment-resistant depression, with electrical current inducing a generalised seizure under anaesthesia. Other biological treatments include TMS, ketamine, and esketamine.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of ECT (electroconvulsive therapy) and other biological treatments?

Key features include: ect mechanism, ect indications, ect efficacy.

Q3. How is ECT (electroconvulsive therapy) and other biological treatments diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for ECT (electroconvulsive therapy) and other biological treatments?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of ECT (electroconvulsive therapy) and other biological treatments?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

  • 'DIG FAST' — mania symptoms
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' — MSE components
  • '4 A's' of capacity — Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate

topics.title: Mood Stabilisers Lithium Valproate Lamotrigine
topics.html:
Definition

Mood stabilisers are medications used to treat bipolar disorder, both for acute episodes (mania, depression) and for long-term prophylaxis. They include lithium, valproate, lamotrigine, and carbamazepine.

Core Clinical Content

Lithium

Gold standard for bipolar; effective in mania, depression, prophylaxis; ↓ suicide risk

Lithium mechanism

Unknown; modulates intracellular signalling, neuroprotection

Lithium monitoring

TFTs (hypothyroidism), U&E (renal), Ca (hyperparathyroidism), levels every 3-6 months

Lithium therapeutic range

0.6-1.0 mmol/L (maintenance); 0.8-1.0 (acute mania); narrow therapeutic index

Lithium toxicity

1.5 mmol/L; tremor, ataxia, confusion, seizures, cardiac; >2.0 = severe; haemodialysis

Lithium side effects

Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, hypothyroidism, tremor, weight gain, teratogenic (Ebstein anomaly)

Valproate

Effective in mania, mixed episodes, rapid cycling; teratogenic (neural tube defects)

Valproate monitoring

LFTs, FBC, level; avoid in women of childbearing age

Lamotrigine

Effective in bipolar depression; needs slow titration (Stevens-Johnson syndrome risk)

Lamotrigine side effects

Rash (incl. Stevens-Johnson), drowsiness, headache; SJS more common with rapid titration

topics.title: Tricyclic Antidepressant Tca Overdose And Serotoni
topics.html:
Definition

TCA overdose is a life-threatening emergency with anticholinergic, cardiovascular, and CNS toxicity. Serotonin discontinuation syndrome is a transient syndrome occurring after abrupt cessation of serotonergic antidepressants, characterised by a constellation of symptoms.

Core Clinical Content

TCA toxicity triad

Anticholinergic, cardiovascular (arrhythmia, hypotension), CNS (seizures, coma)

TCA cardiovascular toxicity

Sodium channel blockade → QRS widening, VT, torsades; hypotension

TCA overdose treatment

Sodium bicarbonate for QRS >100 ms; IV fluids; activated charcoal if <2 h; supportive

TCA lethal dose

~15-20 mg/kg (e.g., 1-1.5 g for adult); 30 mg/kg is often fatal

TCA and serotonin syndrome

TCAs have serotonergic activity; combined with MAOI/SSRI → risk

Serotonin discontinuation syndrome

Occurs after abrupt stop of SSRI/SNRI; dizziness, nausea, paresthesia, 'brain zaps'

Discontinuation timing

Short-half-life SSRIs (paroxetine) worse; fluoxetine less (long half-life)

Discontinuation treatment

Reintroduce antidepressant; taper slowly over weeks-months

Differentiating discontinuation from relapse

Discontinuation: rapid onset, somatic symptoms, brief; relapse: gradual onset, original symptoms return

Discontinuation prevention

Always taper antidepressants gradually; longer taper for short half-life drugs

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome?

TCA overdose is a life-threatening emergency with anticholinergic, cardiovascular, and CNS toxicity. Serotonin discontinuation syndrome is a transient syndrome occurring after abrupt cessation of serotonergic antidepressants, characterised by a constellation of symptoms.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome?

Key features include: tca toxicity triad, tca cardiovascular toxicity, tca overdose treatment.

Q3. How is Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

  • Mnemonic for Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdose and serotonin discontinuation syndrome — see Core Clinical Content key points
  • 'SIG E CAPS' — depression criteria

topics.title: Delusional Disorder
topics.html:
Definition

Delusional disorder is a psychiatric condition characterised by one or more non-bizarre delusions (involving real-life situations that could occur in real life, e.g., persecution, infidelity, illness) lasting ≥1 month, with functioning relatively preserved outside the delusional theme.

Core Clinical Content

Key feature

Non-bizarre delusions (could occur in real life)

Duration

≥1 month

Types

Persecutory, jealous (Othello), erotomanic (de Clerambault), somatic, grandiose, mixed

Erotomanic

False belief that someone (usually higher status) is in love with them

Othello syndrome

Pathological jealousy; belief partner is unfaithful; can lead to violence

Somatic

Belief of having a disease (e.g., parasites, cancer); may seek multiple opinions

Functioning

Relatively preserved outside delusional theme

Behaviour

Generally normal except for actions based on delusion

Treatment

Antipsychotic (poor response); psychotherapy; management of behaviour

Prognosis

Often chronic; insight often limited

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Key featureNon-bizarre delusions (could occur in real life)
Duration≥1 month

ErotomanicFalse belief that someone (usually higher status) is in love with them
Othello syndromePathological jealousy; belief partner is unfaithful; can lead to violence
SomaticBelief of having a disease (e.g., parasites, cancer); may seek multiple opinions
FunctioningRelatively preserved outside delusional theme
BehaviourGenerally normal except for actions based on delusion
TreatmentAntipsychotic (poor response); psychotherapy; management of behaviour
PrognosisOften chronic; insight often limited

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Delusional disorder?

Delusional disorder is a psychiatric condition characterised by one or more non-bizarre delusions (involving real-life situations that could occur in real life, e.g., persecution, infidelity, illness) lasting ≥1 month, with functioning relatively preserved outside the delusional theme.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Delusional disorder?

Key features include: key feature, duration, types. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Delusional disorder diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Delusional disorder?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Delusional disorder?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Delusional disorder — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

topics.title: Schizoaffective Disorder And Related Psychotic Dis
topics.html:
Definition

Schizoaffective disorder is a psychotic disorder with prominent mood symptoms (depressive or manic) coexisting with schizophrenia-like psychotic symptoms, but with ≥2 weeks of psychosis without mood symptoms.

Core Clinical Content

Schizoaffective criteria

Major mood episode (depressive or manic) + ≥2 weeks of psychosis WITHOUT mood symptoms

Distinguishing from schizophrenia

Prominent mood symptoms (not just brief)

Distinguishing from bipolar/ MDD with psychotic features

≥2 weeks of psychosis WITHOUT mood symptoms

Types

Bipolar type (manic + depressive episodes) and depressive type (only depressive episodes)

Schizophreniform disorder

Same as schizophrenia but <6 months duration

Brief psychotic disorder

Psychotic symptoms <1 month with full return to premorbid functioning

Delusional disorder

Non-bizarre delusions (real-life situations) ≥1 month; otherwise functioning preserved

Treatment

Mood stabiliser/antidepressant + antipsychotic; similar to schizophrenia long-term

Prognosis

Better than schizophrenia; worse than mood disorder alone

Schizoaffective prevalence

Rare (~0.3%); often misdiagnosed

A: Prominent mood symptoms (not just brief)

A: ≥2 weeks of psychosis WITHOUT mood symptoms

Q: Types?

A: Bipolar type (manic + depressive episodes) and depressive type (only depressive episodes)

Q: Schizophreniform disorder?

A: Same as schizophrenia but <6 months duration

Q: Brief psychotic disorder?

A: Psychotic symptoms <1 month with full return to premorbid functioning

Q: Delusional disorder?

A: Non-bizarre delusions (real-life situations) ≥1 month; otherwise functioning preserved

Q: Treatment?

A: Mood stabiliser/antidepressant + antipsychotic; similar to schizophrenia long-term

topics.title: Schizophrenia
topics.html:
Definition

Schizophrenia is a chronic psychiatric disorder characterised by positive symptoms (hallucinations, delusions, disorganised speech), negative symptoms (avolition, alogia, flat affect), and cognitive impairment, lasting ≥6 months with ≥1 month of active symptoms.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria

≥2 of 5 symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, disorganised speech, grossly disorganised/catatonic behaviour, negative symptoms); ≥1 must be delusions/hallucinations/disorganised speech

Duration

≥6 months (≥1 month active phase)

Positive symptoms

Hallucinations, delusions, disorganised speech, bizarre behaviour

Negative symptoms

Avolition, alogia, flat affect, anhedonia, asociality

First-rank symptoms (Schneider)

Auditory hallucinations (voices commenting, discussing, thought echo); thought insertion/withdrawal/broadcasting; delusions of control/passivity

Prevalence

Lifetime ~1%; equal M:F; onset M 18-25, F 25-35

Risk factors

Family history (8-10% if first-degree), obstetric complications, cannabis use, urban birth

Dopamine hypothesis

↑ Dopamine in mesolimbic pathway (positive); ↓ in mesocortical (negative)

First-line treatment

Antipsychotic (oral or LAI) + psychosocial support

Suicide risk

~5-10% lifetime; highest in first 5 years and after discharge

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Schizophrenia?

Schizophrenia is a chronic psychiatric disorder characterised by positive symptoms (hallucinations, delusions, disorganised speech), negative symptoms (avolition, alogia, flat affect), and cognitive impairment, lasting ≥6 months with ≥1 month of active symptoms.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Schizophrenia?

Key features include: dsm-5 criteria, duration, positive symptoms. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Schizophrenia diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Schizophrenia?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Schizophrenia?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Schizophrenia — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

topics.title: Gender Dysphoria And Paraphilias
topics.html:
Definition

Gender dysphoria is distress caused by a marked incongruence between experienced/expressed gender and assigned gender, lasting ≥6 months. Paraphilias are persistent, intense, atypical sexual interests; paraphilic disorders cause harm/distress.

Core Clinical Content

Gender dysphoria definition

Distress from incongruence between experienced gender and assigned gender; ≥6 months

Gender dysphoria onset

Often childhood; some present in adolescence/adulthood

GD treatment in children

Reversible interventions (puberty blockers at Tanner stage 2); supportive care

GD treatment in adults

Affirming care; hormones, surgery, psychological support; no conversion therapy

WPATH standards

International guidelines for transgender health; individualised, multi-disciplinary

Paraphilias vs disorders

Paraphilia: atypical interest; paraphilic disorder: causes harm/distress/impairment

Common paraphilias

Paedophilia, exhibitionism, voyeurism, fetishism, frotteurism, sexual masochism/sadism

Paraphilic disorder assessment

Risk assessment (especially for child-related interests); treatment often court-mandated

Treatment

CBT, relapse prevention; anti-androgens or GnRH agonists for severe paraphilias

Co-occurring conditions

Substance use, depression, personality disorders, other paraphilias

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Sexual Dysfunction
topics.html:
Definition

Sexual dysfunctions are a group of disorders characterised by clinically significant disturbance in sexual response or experience, including desire, arousal, orgasm, and pain disorders, causing marked distress.

Core Clinical Content

Types

Desire (low), arousal (erectile, female), orgasm (premature, delayed, anorgasmia), pain (dyspareunia, vaginismus)

Erectile dysfunction (ED)

Inability to achieve/maintain erection; organic vs psychogenic causes

ED organic causes

Vascular (atherosclerosis), diabetes, hormonal, neurological, medications, alcohol

ED psychogenic causes

Anxiety (performance), depression, relationship problems, stress

ED investigations

Testosterone, prolactin, glucose, lipids, TSH; nocturnal penile tumescence test

ED treatment

PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil) first-line; vacuum device; intracavernosal injection; implant

Premature ejaculation

Ejaculation sooner than desired; behavioural therapy, dapoxetine (SSRI)

Vaginismus

Involuntary vaginal muscle contraction preventing penetration; pelvic floor therapy, CBT

Anorgasmia in women

Often psychological; ensure adequate stimulation, address anxiety

Sexual dysfunction in psychiatric illness

Common in depression, anxiety, PTSD; screen routinely

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Sexual dysfunctions are a group of disorders characterised by clinically significant disturbance in sexual response or experience, including desire, arousal, orgasm, and pain disorders, causing marked distress.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Sexual dysfunction?

Key features include: types, erectile dysfunction (ed), ed organic causes.

topics.title: Insomnia Disorder
topics.html:
Definition

Insomnia disorder is a sleep-wake disorder characterised by dissatisfaction with sleep quantity or quality, with difficulty initiating sleep, maintaining sleep, or early morning awakening, causing significant daytime impairment, occurring ≥3 nights/week for ≥3 months.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria

Dissatisfaction with sleep quantity/quality; ≥3 nights/week for ≥3 months; daytime impairment; not better explained by another condition

Types

Sleep-onset, sleep-maintenance, early-morning awakening, mixed

Prevalence

Common (~30% adults have symptoms; 10% chronic)

Causes

Stress, medical conditions, psychiatric disorders, medications, substance use, poor sleep hygiene

3P model

Predisposing, Precipitating, Perpetuating factors

Sleep hygiene

Regular schedule, dark/cool room, no caffeine/alcohol/exercise before bed, no screens

CBT-I

Cognitive behavioural therapy for insomnia; first-line; lasting benefit

CBT-I components

Stimulus control, sleep restriction, relaxation, cognitive therapy, sleep hygiene

Pharmacotherapy

Short-term only: Z-drugs (zolpidem, zopiclone) or melatonin; AVOID long-term benzodiazepines

Dependence risk

High with benzodiazepines and Z-drugs; reserved for short-term use

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Obstructive Sleep Apnoea And Narcolepsy
topics.html:
Definition

Obstructive sleep apnoea (OSA) is a sleep-related breathing disorder characterised by repetitive upper airway collapse during sleep causing apnoeas/hypopnoeas, oxygen desaturation, and sleep fragmentation. Narcolepsy is a central hypersomnolence disorder with excessive daytime sleepiness, cataplexy, sleep paralysis, and hypnagogic hallucinations.

Core Clinical Content

OSA definition

Repetitive upper airway collapse during sleep with apnoea/hypopnoea

OSA risk factors

Obesity, male, age, large neck, alcohol, sedative use, anatomical (enlarged tonsils)

OSA symptoms

Snoring, witnessed apnoeas, daytime sleepiness, morning headache, poor concentration

OSA diagnosis

Polysomnography; AHI ≥5 (mild), ≥15 (moderate), ≥30 (severe)

OSA complications

Hypertension, MI, stroke, accidents, type 2 diabetes, depression

OSA treatment

CPAP first-line; weight loss; positional therapy; mandibular advancement device; surgery (UPPP)

Narcolepsy tetrad

Excessive daytime sleepiness, cataplexy, sleep paralysis, hypnagogic hallucinations

Narcolepsy type 1

With cataplexy (loss of muscle tone with emotion); low hypocretin

Narcolepsy type 2

Without cataplexy

Narcolepsy treatment

Modafinil (first-line); sodium oxybate (for cataplexy); modafinil, methylphenidate

  1. Patient presents with features of Obstructive sleep apnoea and narcolepsy. Which is most appropriate? | Excessive daytime sleepiness, cataplexy, sleep paralysis, hypnagogic hallucinations

Flashcards

Q: OSA definition?

A: Repetitive upper airway collapse during sleep with apnoea/hypopnoea

Q: OSA risk factors?

A: Obesity, male, age, large neck, alcohol, sedative use, anatomical (enlarged tonsils)

Q: OSA symptoms?

A: Snoring, witnessed apnoeas, daytime sleepiness, morning headache, poor concentration

Q: OSA diagnosis?

A: Polysomnography; AHI ≥5 (mild), ≥15 (moderate), ≥30 (severe)

Q: OSA complications?

A: Hypertension, MI, stroke, accidents, type 2 diabetes, depression

Q: OSA treatment?

A: CPAP first-line; weight loss; positional therapy; mandibular advancement device; surgery (UPPP)

Q: Narcolepsy tetrad?

A: Excessive daytime sleepiness, cataplexy, sleep paralysis, hypnagogic hallucinations

Q: Narcolepsy type 1?

A: With cataplexy (loss of muscle tone with emotion); low hypocretin

topics.title: Other Sleep Disorders Parasomnias Rbd Restless Leg
topics.html:
Definition

Other sleep disorders include parasomnias (abnormal behaviours during sleep — NREM and REM), REM sleep behaviour disorder (RBD), and restless legs syndrome (RLS), each with distinct features and management.

Core Clinical Content

Parasomnias

Abnormal behaviours/emotions during sleep (NREM: sleepwalking, terrors; REM: REM behaviour disorder)

Sleepwalking (somnambulism)

NREM parasomnia; complex behaviour during deep sleep; amnesia

Sleep terrors

NREM parasomnia; screaming, autonomic arousal, no memory; common in children

REM sleep behaviour disorder (RBD)

Loss of normal REM atonia; acting out dreams; risk of injury

RBD association

Strong marker for α-synucleinopathies (Parkinson's, DLB, MSA)

RBD treatment

Clonazepam, melatonin; bed safety

Restless legs syndrome (RLS)

Urge to move legs, worse at rest/evening, relieved by movement

RLS associations

Iron deficiency (low ferritin), pregnancy, CKD, peripheral neuropathy

RLS treatment

Iron replacement if ferritin <75; dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole); gabapentin

Night terrors vs nightmares

Night terrors: NREM, no recall; nightmares: REM, vivid recall

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Adult Adhd And Tic Disorders
topics.html:
Definition

Adult attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder persisting into adulthood, characterised by inattention, hyperactivity, and impulsivity. Tic disorders, including Tourette syndrome, are characterised by sudden, rapid, recurrent motor movements or vocalisations.

Core Clinical Content

Adult ADHD prevalence

~3-5% of adults; 50% of childhood ADHD persists into adulthood

ADHD triad

Inattention, hyperactivity, impulsivity; ICD-11 also includes emotional dysregulation

Adult presentation

Inattention predominates; hyperactivity less prominent; emotional dysregulation

Comorbidities

Anxiety, depression, substance use, OCD, personality disorders, sleep disorders

Diagnosis

Clinical; DIVA-5 or ACE+; collateral history important; consider QB tests

Treatment

Stimulants (methylphenidate, lisdexamfetamine) first-line; atomoxetine (non-stimulant)

Stimulant side effects

Appetite ↓, weight loss, insomnia, ↑ HR/BP, tics; abuse potential

Atomoxetine

Non-stimulant; less effective; useful in substance use comorbidity

Tourette syndrome

Multiple motor + ≥1 vocal tics; >1 year; onset <18; ADHD 50%

Tourette treatment

Behavioural therapy first; α2 agonists (clonidine, guanfacine); antipsychotics for severe

topics.title: Autism Spectrum Disorder And Intellectual Disabili
topics.html:
Definition

Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder characterised by persistent deficits in social communication and interaction plus restricted, repetitive patterns of behaviour, interests, or activities. Intellectual disability (ID) is characterised by significant limitations in intellectual functioning and adaptive behaviour, with onset in the developmental period.

Core Clinical Content

ASD prevalence

~1-2% of children; 4:1 male:female ratio

ASD triad (DSM-5)

Persistent deficits in: (1) social communication/interaction, (2) restricted/repetitive behaviours, (3) early onset

ASD onset

Recognisable in early childhood; some regression in language/social skills

ASD severity levels

Level 1: requiring support; Level 2: substantial support; Level 3: very substantial support

ASD comorbidities

Intellectual disability (~30%), ADHD, anxiety, depression, OCD, tics, epilepsy

ASD causes

Mostly genetic; specific genes (SHANK3, CHD8, FMR1); environmental (advanced parental age)

ASD management

Early intervention; ABA (applied behaviour analysis); speech therapy; OT; school support; SSRI for OCD/anxiety

ASD pharmacotherapy

No curative medication; antipsychotic (risperidone, aripiprazole) for irritability; melatonin for sleep

Intellectual disability (ID)

IQ <70; deficits in adaptive functioning; onset <18 years

ID severity

Mild (IQ 50-69), moderate (35-49), severe (20-34), profound (<20)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Q1. What is the definition of Autism spectrum disorder and intellectual disability?

Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder characterised by persistent deficits in social communication and interaction plus restricted, repetitive patterns of behaviour, interests, or activities. Intellectual disability (ID) is characterised by significant limitations in intellectual functioning and adaptive behaviour, with onset in the developmental period.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Autism spectrum disorder and intellectual disability?

Key features include: asd prevalence, asd triad (dsm-5), asd onset.

Q3. How is Autism spectrum disorder and intellectual disability diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Autism spectrum disorder and intellectual disability?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Autism spectrum disorder and intellectual disability?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

topics.title: Child And Adolescent Psychiatry Overview
topics.html:
Definition

Child and adolescent psychiatry addresses mental health conditions in children and adolescents, with disorders often presenting differently from adults and requiring developmentally appropriate assessment and treatment.

Core Clinical Content

ADHD

Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder; 5% children; inattentive/hyperactive/combined; stimulants first-line

Autism spectrum disorder (ASD)

Persistent deficits in social communication + restricted/repetitive behaviours; early onset

Conduct disorder

Repetitive violation of others' rights/age-appropriate norms; <18 years; precursor to ASPD

Oppositional defiant disorder (ODD)

Angry/irritable mood, argumentative, vindictive behaviour; less severe than CD

Tourette syndrome

Multiple motor + ≥1 vocal tics; >1 year; onset <18; ADHD comorbidity 50%

Specific learning disorder

Persistent difficulties in reading (dyslexia), writing, mathematics

Enuresis

Involuntary urination ≥2/week in ≥5-year-old; primary vs secondary; enuresis alarm first-line

Encopresis

Faecal incontinence; usually functional; consider abuse if secondary

Adolescent depression

Often masked (irritability, somatic); ↑ suicide risk; SSRIs (fluoxetine approved)

Eating disorders in adolescents

Family-based treatment (FBT) first-line; medical stabilisation priority

topics.title: Conduct Disorder And Oppositional Defiant Disorder
topics.html:
Definition

Conduct disorder (CD) is characterised by repetitive and persistent patterns of behaviour that violate the basic rights of others or major age-appropriate societal norms. Oppositional defiant disorder (ODD) is a pattern of angry/irritable mood, argumentative/defiant behaviour, or vindictiveness lasting ≥6 months, less severe than CD.

Core Clinical Content

ODD features

Angry/irritable mood, argumentative/defiant behaviour, vindictiveness ≥6 months

ODD prevalence

~3-5% of children; equal M:F before puberty; more males after

ODD vs CD

ODD: defiance, hostility, no major rights violations; CD: violates others' rights/norms

Conduct disorder (CD)

Aggression to people/animals, destruction of property, deceitfulness/theft, serious rule violation

CD prevalence

~4-5% of children; 3-4x more common in males

CD course

Often persists; precursor to antisocial personality disorder in adulthood (≥18 years)

CD risk factors

Genetic (heritability ~50%), neurobiological (↓ MAO-A), family/parenting, socioeconomic, peer

Comorbidities

ADHD (50%), depression, anxiety, substance use, learning disorders

Treatment

Parent management training (PMT) - first-line; cognitive problem-solving; multi-systemic therapy; SSRI for aggression

Prognosis

Better in ODD (1/3 remit); worse in CD with high callous-unemotional traits (poor prognosis)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Conduct disorder and oppositional defiant disorder in children?

topics.title: Old Age Psychiatry And Dementia Syndromes
topics.html:
Definition

Old age psychiatry addresses mental health in older adults, including dementia, late-life depression, anxiety, psychosis, and behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia (BPSD). Comorbid physical illness and polypharmacy are common considerations.

Core Clinical Content

Late-life depression

Common but underdiagnosed; often somatic; may present as pseudodementia

Pseudodementia

Cognitive impairment from depression; reversible; differs from dementia (depressed says 'I don't know')

BPSD

Behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia; non-cognitive symptoms

BPSD features

Agitation, aggression, psychosis, depression, wandering, sleep disturbance

BPSD non-pharmacological

Person-centred care, environment, activities, communication strategies

BPSD pharmacological

Antipsychotic (risperidone, haloperidol) only if severe/aggressive; risk of cerebrovascular events

Antipsychotic in dementia

↑ Mortality, ↑ CVA risk; only use after non-pharmacological, shortest time, regular review

Depression in elderly

SSRI first-line; avoid TCAs (anticholinergic, falls); longer time to response

Sleep in elderly

Reduced need, fragmented; non-pharmacological first; melatonin may help

Polypharmacy

Common; review regularly; anticholinergic burden affects cognition

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Old age psychiatry addresses mental health in older adults, including dementia, late-life depression, anxiety, psychosis, and behavioural and psychological symptoms of dementia (BPSD). Comorbid physical illness and polypharmacy are common considerations.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Old age psychiatry and dementia syndromes?

Key features include: late-life depression, pseudodementia, bpsd.

Q3. How is Old age psychiatry and dementia syndromes diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Old age psychiatry and dementia syndromes?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Old age psychiatry and dementia syndromes?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

topics.title: Psychiatric Aspects Of Physical Illness
topics.html:
Definition

Psychiatric conditions frequently co-occur with physical illness, either as a consequence of the medical condition, its treatment, or as an independent comorbidity. Recognition and management are essential for outcomes.

Core Clinical Content

Cardiovascular disease

Depression ↑ mortality in post-MI; SSRI (sertraline) safe; caution with TCAs (cardiotoxic)

Stroke

Post-stroke depression 30-40%; peaks 3-6 months; often missed; treat with SSRI

Cancer

Depression in 20-40%; screen with PHQ-9; SSRI first-line; avoid in tamoxifen (paroxetine, fluoxetine)

Diabetes

Depression ↑ risk; ↑ poor control; consider SSRI

Parkinson's disease

Depression in 40-50%; SSRI (caution with tremor); avoid antipsychotics (EPS)

Epilepsy

Depression ↑ suicide risk; avoid TCAs, lowering seizure threshold; consider CBT

HIV/AIDS

Depression, anxiety, psychosis; high suicide risk; screen all HIV patients

Endocrine

Hypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, Cushing's, Addison's — all can present with psychiatric symptoms

Vitamin deficiencies

B12, folate, thiamine (Wernicke), vitamin D — all linked to depression/cognition

Steroid-induced psychosis

High-dose corticosteroids; treat by reducing dose; antipsychotic if needed

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Psychiatric Aspects Of Pregnancy And Postpartum
topics.html:
Definition

Perinatal psychiatry addresses mental health conditions occurring during pregnancy (antenatal) and up to one year postpartum (postnatal). Conditions include baby blues, postpartum depression, puerperal psychosis, and pre-existing psychiatric conditions in pregnancy.

Core Clinical Content

Antenatal depression

10-15% of pregnancies; often underdiagnosed; risk factors: previous depression, lack of support

Baby blues

50-80%; 3-5 days postpartum; mild; resolves by 10-14 days

Postpartum depression

10-15%; within 4 weeks (DSM-5) or up to 12 months; same criteria as MDD

Puerperal psychosis

1-2 per 1000; psychiatric emergency; within 2 weeks; high suicide/infanticide risk

Treatment in pregnancy

SSRI (sertraline preferred); avoid paroxetine (cardiac malformations); ECT if severe

Treatment in breastfeeding

Sertraline preferred (lowest infant levels); avoid lithium, benzodiazepines, lamotrigine

Antidepressant in pregnancy

Risks: persistent pulmonary hypertension of newborn, neonatal adaptation syndrome

Valproate in pregnancy

HIGHLY teratogenic (neural tube defects); AVOID in women of childbearing age

Lithium in pregnancy

Ebstein anomaly (cardiac malformation); avoid in 1st trimester; use cautiously

Perinatal mental health team

Joint obstetric-psychiatric care; mother-baby unit if needed

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Perinatal psychiatry addresses mental health conditions occurring during pregnancy (antenatal) and up to one year postpartum (postnatal). Conditions include baby blues, postpartum depression, puerperal psychosis, and pre-existing psychiatric conditions in pregnancy.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Psychiatric aspects of pregnancy and postpartum?

Key features include: antenatal depression, baby blues, postpartum depression.

Q3. How is Psychiatric aspects of pregnancy and postpartum diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Psychiatric aspects of pregnancy and postpartum?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Psychiatric aspects of pregnancy and postpartum?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.
