topics.title: Specific Learning Disorders And Enuresis
topics.html:
Definition

Specific learning disorders are neurodevelopmental disorders characterised by persistent difficulties in learning academic skills, with onset in school age. Enuresis is involuntary urination in a child ≥5 years old; encopresis is involuntary faecal soiling. Both are common in childhood and often have psychological components.

Core Clinical Content

Specific learning disorder types

Dyslexia (reading), dyscalculia (maths), dysgraphia (writing)

Dyslexia prevalence

~5-10% of school-age children; M:F 2:1; phonological processing deficit

Dyslexia features

Letter/word reversal, slow reading, poor spelling, phoneme awareness problems

Dyscalculia prevalence

~3-6%; difficulty with numbers, arithmetic, mathematical reasoning

Dyslexia diagnosis

Achievement below expected for age + IQ + education; psychometric testing

Dyslexia management

Phonics-based reading instruction; educational support; screen for comorbidities (ADHD, anxiety)

Enuresis definition

Involuntary urination ≥2/week in ≥5-year-old; ≥3 months

Enuresis types

Primary (never dry) vs secondary (dry then relapses); monosymptomatic vs non-monosymptomatic

Enuresis causes

Genetic, maturational delay, small bladder, ↑ ADH, psychological, UTI, sleep apnoea

Enuresis treatment

Enuresis alarm (first-line, most effective); desmopressin; reward systems; address comorbidities

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Specific learning disorders and enuresis?

Q3. How is Specific learning disorders and enuresis diagnosed?

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Specific learning disorders and enuresis?

Q5. What is the prognosis of Specific learning disorders and enuresis?

topics.title: Alcohol Use Disorder
topics.html:
Definition

Alcohol use disorder (AUD) is a chronic, relapsing condition characterised by problematic alcohol use causing significant impairment or distress, with ≥2 of 11 DSM-5 criteria over 12 months.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria

≥2 of 11 in 12 months (tolerance, withdrawal, larger amounts, unsuccessful cut-down, time spent, role impairment, social impairment, hazardous use, use despite problems, craving)

Screening tools

AUDIT (10-item), CAGE (4-item)

CAGE

Cut down, Annoyed, Guilty, Eye-opener

UK alcohol limits

≤14 units/week for both M and F (revised 2016)

Complications

Hepatic, neurological (Wernicke, Korsakoff), cardiac, pancreatitis, malignancy

Wernicke's encephalopathy

Triad: confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia

Korsakoff syndrome

Anterograde amnesia, confabulation, irreversible

Treatment of dependence

Detoxification, relapse prevention (naltrexone, acamprosate, disulfiram)

Naltrexone

μ-opioid antagonist; reduces craving; monitor LFTs

Acamprosate

NMDA antagonist; reduces craving; safe in liver disease

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

DSM-5 criteria≥2 of 11 in 12 months (tolerance, withdrawal, larger amounts, unsuccessful cut-d

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Alcohol use disorder?

Alcohol use disorder (AUD) is a chronic, relapsing condition characterised by problematic alcohol use causing significant impairment or distress, with ≥2 of 11 DSM-5 criteria over 12 months.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Alcohol use disorder?

Key features include: dsm-5 criteria, screening tools, cage. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Alcohol use disorder diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Alcohol use disorder?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Alcohol use disorder?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Alcohol use disorder — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

topics.title: Alcohol Withdrawal And Delirium Tremens
topics.html:
Definition

Alcohol withdrawal is a clinical syndrome that develops 6-24 hours after cessation in dependent drinkers, ranging from mild tremor to severe delirium tremens (DTs) with autonomic hyperactivity, confusion, and seizures.

Core Clinical Content

Minor withdrawal

6-24 h after last drink: tremor, anxiety, insomnia, GI upset, autonomic hyperactivity

Seizures

12-48 h; generalised tonic-clonic; usually self-limiting

Alcoholic hallucinosis

12-24 h; visual/auditory hallucinations with clear sensorium

Delirium tremens (DTs)

48-96 h (up to 5 days); confusion, autonomic hyperactivity, hallucinations, tremor

DTs mortality

5-15% untreated; <1% with treatment

Treatment

Long-acting benzodiazepine (chlordiazepoxide, diazepam) — symptom-triggered or fixed dose

CIWA-Ar

Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol; guides dosing

Thiamine

BEFORE glucose; Pabrinex IV for Wernicke's prevention

Pabrinex

IV thiamine + pyridoxine; give before glucose to prevent Wernicke's

Seizure prophylaxis

Not routinely; treat seizures with benzodiazepine; avoid phenytoin (ineffective)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Q1. What is the definition of Alcohol withdrawal and delirium tremens?

Alcohol withdrawal is a clinical syndrome that develops 6-24 hours after cessation in dependent drinkers, ranging from mild tremor to severe delirium tremens (DTs) with autonomic hyperactivity, confusion, and seizures.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Alcohol withdrawal and delirium tremens?

Key features include: minor withdrawal, seizures, alcoholic hallucinosis. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Alcohol withdrawal and delirium tremens diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Alcohol withdrawal and delirium tremens?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Alcohol withdrawal and delirium tremens?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

topics.title: Benzodiazepine And Z Drug Use Disorder
topics.html:
Definition

Benzodiazepine and Z-drug use disorder is a chronic condition characterised by problematic use of these sedative-hypnotic agents, with significant risk of dependence and withdrawal that can be life-threatening.

Core Clinical Content

Mechanism

Benzodiazepines: ↑ GABA-A; Z-drugs: similar but more selective for α1 subunit

Common drugs

Benzodiazepines (diazepam, lorazepam, temazepam); Z-drugs (zolpidem, zopiclone, zaleplon)

Indication for long-term use

Limited; only for refractory epilepsy, severe anxiety unresponsive to other treatments

Tolerance

Develops over weeks to months

Withdrawal symptoms

Rebound anxiety, insomnia, tremor, autonomic hyperactivity, perceptual disturbance

Severe withdrawal

Seizures, delirium, similar to alcohol withdrawal

Withdrawal timing

Short-acting (lorazepam): 1-2 days onset; long-acting (diazepam): 5-7 days onset

Withdrawal management

Convert to long-acting (diazepam), then taper slowly (5-10% every 1-2 weeks)

Elderly

Increased sensitivity, falls, cognitive impairment; AVOID long-term

Overdose

Reversed with flumazenil (cautiously — risk of seizures in dependent users)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Q: Mechanism?

A: Benzodiazepines: ↑ GABA-A; Z-drugs: similar but more selective for α1 subunit

Q: Common drugs?

A: Benzodiazepines (diazepam, lorazepam, temazepam); Z-drugs (zolpidem, zopiclone, zaleplon)

Q: Indication for long-term use?

A: Limited; only for refractory epilepsy, severe anxiety unresponsive to other treatments

Q: Tolerance?

A: Develops over weeks to months

Q: Withdrawal symptoms?

A: Rebound anxiety, insomnia, tremor, autonomic hyperactivity, perceptual disturbance

Q: Severe withdrawal?

A: Seizures, delirium, similar to alcohol withdrawal

Q: Withdrawal timing?

A: Short-acting (lorazepam): 1-2 days onset; long-acting (diazepam): 5-7 days onset

Q: Withdrawal management?

A: Convert to long-acting (diazepam), then taper slowly (5-10% every 1-2 weeks)

topics.title: Gambling Disorder And Behavioural Addictions
topics.html:
Definition

Gambling disorder is characterised by persistent, recurrent problematic gambling behaviour leading to significant impairment or distress. Behavioural addictions (also called 'process addictions' or 'addictions without substance') include gambling, gaming, internet, food, sex, shopping, and exercise, with similar features to substance use disorders.

Core Clinical Content

Gambling disorder DSM-5

Persistent problematic gambling; ≥4 of 9 criteria in 12 months (similar to SUD)

Gambling prevalence

~1-2%; more common in males; adolescence and young adults

Gambling types

Pathological gambling (behavioural), problem gambling (mild)

Gambling risk factors

Impulsivity, depression, anxiety, substance use, family history, accessibility

Gambling comorbidities

Depression (50%), anxiety, substance use, suicide risk ↑↑

Gambling treatment

CBT (most evidence), motivational interviewing, Gamblers Anonymous, naltrexone/opioid antagonists

Internet gaming disorder (ICD-11)

Persistent gaming despite harm; impaired control; prioritised over other activities

Other behavioural addictions

Compulsive sexual behaviour, binge eating, shopping, exercise

Compulsive sexual behaviour (ICD-11)

Persistent failure to control sexual urges; significant distress/impairment

Common features of behavioural addictions

Salience, mood modification, tolerance, withdrawal, conflict, relapse

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Gambling disorder and behavioural addictions?

Key features include: gambling disorder dsm-5, gambling prevalence, gambling types.

Q3. How is Gambling disorder and behavioural addictions diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Gambling disorder and behavioural addictions?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Gambling disorder and behavioural addictions?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

topics.title: Opioid Use Disorder And Overdose
topics.html:
Definition

Opioid use disorder is a chronic relapsing condition characterised by problematic opioid use, with opioid overdose being a life-threatening emergency requiring urgent reversal with naloxone.

Core Clinical Content

Common opioids

Heroin, morphine, oxycodone, fentanyl, methadone, codeine, tramadol

Overdose triad

Respiratory depression, pinpoint pupils, decreased consciousness (NOT miosis alone)

Other overdose signs

Hypoxia, bradycardia, hypotension, hypothermia

Naloxone

μ-opioid antagonist; rapid onset (1-2 min); short-acting (30-90 min)

Repeat naloxone

May need repeated doses as naloxone wears off before opioid

Naloxone administration

IV/IM/SC/intranasal; start at 0.4-2 mg, titrate to respiratory effort

Duration monitoring

At least 4-6 hours after last naloxone dose (longer for long-acting opioids)

Methadone

Long-acting opioid agonist; risk of QTc prolongation; used in maintenance therapy

Buprenorphine

Partial μ-agonist; ceiling effect (safer); used in maintenance therapy

Withdrawal

Yawning, lacrimation, piloerection, mydriasis, GI upset, myalgia; not life-threatening

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Opioid use disorder and overdose?

Opioid use disorder is a chronic relapsing condition characterised by problematic opioid use, with opioid overdose being a life-threatening emergency requiring urgent reversal with naloxone.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Opioid use disorder and overdose?

Key features include: common opioids, overdose triad, other overdose signs. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Opioid use disorder and overdose diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Opioid use disorder and overdose?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Opioid use disorder and overdose?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Opioid use disorder and overdose — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

topics.title: Stimulant And Other Substance Use Disorders
topics.html:
Definition

Stimulant and other substance use disorders involve problematic use of substances including cocaine, amphetamines, cannabis, hallucinogens, and tobacco, with significant health, social, and psychological consequences.

Core Clinical Content

Cocaine

Blocks dopamine, noradrenaline, serotonin reuptake; intense euphoria followed by crash

Cocaine withdrawal

Depression, fatigue, hypersomnia, craving (not life-threatening)

Cocaine complications

MI, stroke, aortic dissection, arrhythmia, seizures, nasal septum perforation

Amphetamines

Similar to cocaine; longer duration; risk of psychosis (paranoid, tactile hallucinations)

Cannabis

Most used illicit drug; acute effects: euphoria, anxiety, paranoia, perceptual changes

Cannabis withdrawal

Irritability, anxiety, insomnia, anorexia; mild

Cannabis and psychosis

Dose-related risk of schizophrenia-spectrum disorders; earlier onset

Hallucinogens (LSD, psilocybin)

Perceptual disturbances, synaesthesia; bad trips, flashbacks (HPPD)

Tobacco

Leading preventable cause of death; nicotine replacement, varenicline, bupropion for cessation

Volatile substance abuse

Solvents, glue; acute: euphoria, hallucinations; chronic: neurotoxicity, peripheral neuropathy

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TobaccoLeading preventable cause of death; nicotine replacement, varenicline, bupropion

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Stimulant and other substance use disorders?

Stimulant and other substance use disorders involve problematic use of substances including cocaine, amphetamines, cannabis, hallucinogens, and tobacco, with significant health, social, and psychological consequences.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Stimulant and other substance use disorders?

Key features include: cocaine, cocaine withdrawal, cocaine complications. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Stimulant and other substance use disorders diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Stimulant and other substance use disorders?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Stimulant and other substance use disorders?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

  • Mnemonic for Stimulant and other substance use disorders — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

topics.title: Non Suicidal Self Injury Nssi And Postvention
topics.html:
Definition

Non-suicidal self-injury (NSSI) is intentional self-damage (cutting, burning, hitting) without suicidal intent, often used for emotional regulation. Postvention refers to interventions after a suicide to support affected individuals and prevent further suicides.

Core Clinical Content

NSSI definition

Intentional self-damage without suicidal intent; ≥5 days in past year for DSM-5 diagnosis

NSSI prevalence

17% in adolescents; 5% in adults; more common in females

Common methods

Cutting, burning, scratching, hitting self

Functions of NSSI

Affect regulation, self-punishment, anti-dissociation, interpersonal communication

Risk factors

Childhood trauma, BPD, depression, eating disorders, substance use

Differential from suicide attempt

NSSI: no intent to die; often for relief; usually superficial

Differential from BD

NSSI: not suicidal; BD: suicidal; can co-occur

Management

DBT (first-line for NSSI/BPD), CBT, addressing underlying distress

Postvention

Support for bereaved after suicide; psychoeducation; reducing contagion

Papageno effect vs Werther effect

Papageno: protective media (recovery stories); Werther: imitative suicide after media coverage

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Suicide Risk Assessment And Prevention
topics.html:
Definition

Suicide is the act of intentionally ending one's own life. Suicide risk assessment is a structured evaluation of factors that increase or decrease suicide risk, used to guide safety planning and intervention. Self-harm (non-suicidal self-injury, NSSI) is intentional self-damage without suicidal intent.

Core Clinical Content

Suicide epidemiology

~700,000 deaths/year worldwide; 1 every 40 seconds; leading cause of death in young adults

Risk factors (SAD PERSONS)

Sex (M), Age (extremes), Depression, Previous attempt, Ethanol, Rationality loss, Social support lacking, Organised plan, No spouse, Sickness

Strongest risk factor

Previous suicide attempt (40x increased risk)

Protective factors

Family support, religious beliefs, engagement with treatment, reasons for living, access to care

Warning signs

Talking about wanting to die, looking for means, withdrawal, mood changes, giving possessions

Direct questioning

Asking about suicide does NOT increase risk; can decrease it

Suicide plan

Specificity of plan, availability of means, timeframe all increase risk

Self-harm (NSSI)

Intentional self-injury without suicidal intent; often for emotional regulation

Treatment

Safety plan, remove means, CBT-DBT, medications, hospitalisation if high risk

Means restriction

Effective prevention strategy (e.g., bridge barriers, medication blister packs)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Self-harm (non-suicidal self-injury, NSSI) is intentional self-damage without suicidal intent.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Suicide risk assessment and prevention?

Key features include: suicide epidemiology, risk factors (sad persons), strongest risk factor.

Q3. How is Suicide risk assessment and prevention diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Suicide risk assessment and prevention?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Suicide risk assessment and prevention?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

topics.title: Bereavement Grief And Adjustment Disorders
topics.html:
Definition

Bereavement is the loss of a loved one through death. Grief is the normal emotional response to loss. Adjustment disorder is a maladaptive response to an identifiable stressor occurring within 3 months, with symptoms that resolve within 6 months of stressor removal.

Core Clinical Content

Normal grief stages (Kübler-Ross)

Denial, anger, bargaining, depression, acceptance (note: not linear)

Worden's tasks of mourning

Accept reality, work through pain, adjust to life without deceased, find lasting connection

Anticipatory grief

Before the loss; common in terminal illness

Prolonged grief disorder (PGD)

Grief >12 months; yearning for deceased; functional impairment; new DSM-5 diagnosis

Complicated grief

Severe, prolonged grief; may need intervention

Bereavement vs MDD

MDD: pervasive depression, anhedonia, suicidal ideation, low self-esteem; grief: waves, focused on deceased

Adjustment disorder subtypes

Depressed mood, anxiety, mixed, disturbance of conduct

Adjustment disorder criteria

Symptoms within 3 months of stressor; not meeting criteria for another disorder; resolves within 6 months of stressor removal

Treatment of complicated grief

CBT, complicated grief therapy, support groups; avoid routine antidepressants

Pharmacological considerations

AVOID benzodiazepines; short-term hypnotics for sleep; SSRI for comorbid depression

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Bereavement, grief, and adjustment disorders?

Bereavement is the loss of a loved one through death. Grief is the normal emotional response to loss. Adjustment disorder is a maladaptive response to an identifiable stressor occurring within 3 months, with symptoms that resolve within 6 months of stressor removal.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Bereavement, grief, and adjustment disorders?

Key features include: normal grief stages (kübler-ross), worden's tasks of mourning, anticipatory grief.

Q3. How is Bereavement, grief, and adjustment disorders diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Bereavement, grief, and adjustment disorders?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Bereavement, grief, and adjustment disorders?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

topics.title: Chronic Pain And Medically Unexplained Pain
topics.html:
Definition

Chronic pain is pain persisting >3 months, often with limited response to standard treatments. It involves complex interactions between physical, psychological, and social factors (biopsychosocial model). Medically unexplained pain (somatic symptom disorder with predominant pain) overlaps with this.

Core Clinical Content

Chronic pain definition

Pain persisting >3 months; >normal healing time

Biopsychosocial model

Pain = interaction of biological, psychological (anxiety, depression, catastrophising), social factors

Yellow flags

Psychosocial risk factors for chronicity: beliefs, emotions, behaviours, social context

Depression in chronic pain

30-50% comorbidity; bidirectional relationship; under-recognised

Catastrophising

Negative cognitive style ('this is terrible, will never end'); predicts pain outcomes

Opioid use

Limited benefit beyond 4-6 weeks; high risk dependence; AVOID long-term

Gabapentinoids

Gabapentin, pregabalin; for neuropathic pain; risk of dependence, sedation

Tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs)

Amitriptyline, nortriptyline; for neuropathic pain, chronic headache; start low

SNRIs

Duloxetine; for neuropathic pain (diabetic), fibromyalgia, chronic musculoskeletal pain

Multidisciplinary pain management

Combination of medication, physiotherapy, CBT, occupational therapy, self-management

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Chronic pain and medically unexplained pain?

Q3. How is Chronic pain and medically unexplained pain diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Chronic pain and medically unexplained pain?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Chronic pain and medically unexplained pain?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

topics.title: Functional Disorders And Stress Related Illness
topics.html:
Definition

Functional disorders (medically unexplained symptoms) are conditions with distressing symptoms not explained by organic disease, often triggered or exacerbated by stress, with significant functional impact. They are managed with the biopsychosocial model and avoiding excessive investigation.

Core Clinical Content

Functional disorders

Symptoms not explained by organic disease; real, not 'faked'

Common functional disorders

Fibromyalgia, IBS, chronic fatigue syndrome, functional dyspepsia, non-cardiac chest pain

Fibromyalgia

Widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, sleep disturbance, tender points

Fibromyalgia treatment

Exercise (most effective), CBT, duloxetine, pregabalin; multidisciplinary

Chronic fatigue syndrome (CFS/ME)

Severe fatigue >6 months, post-exertional malaise, unrefreshing sleep, cognitive dysfunction

CFS/ME management

Pacing, activity management, CBT, supportive care; avoid excessive rest

Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)

Abdominal pain, altered bowel habit; consider as functional if no organic cause

Functional neurological disorder (FND)

See Conversion disorder (separate file)

Stress-related illness

Adjustment disorder, acute stress reaction; often self-limiting

Medically unexplained symptoms (MUS)

20-30% of primary care consultations; avoid dismissive 'all in the head' approach

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Q1. What is the definition of Functional disorders and stress-related illness?

Functional disorders (medically unexplained symptoms) are conditions with distressing symptoms not explained by organic disease, often triggered or exacerbated by stress, with significant functional impact. They are managed with the biopsychosocial model and avoiding excessive investigation.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Functional disorders and stress-related illness?

Key features include: functional disorders, common functional disorders, fibromyalgia.

Q3. How is Functional disorders and stress-related illness diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Functional disorders and stress-related illness?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Functional disorders and stress-related illness?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

topics.title: Alzheimer S Disease And Other Dementias
topics.html:
Definition

Alzheimer's disease (AD) is the most common form of dementia (60-70%), characterised by progressive memory impairment, other cognitive deficits, and behavioural changes, with characteristic neuropathology of amyloid plaques and neurofibrillary tangles.

Core Clinical Content

Dementia definition

Acquired cognitive decline in ≥2 domains severe enough to impair function

Alzheimer's prevalence

60-70% of all dementia; 10% of >65 years; 30% of >85 years

Pathology

Amyloid (Aβ) plaques + neurofibrillary tangles (hyperphosphorylated tau) + cortical atrophy

Risk factors

Age, APOE4, family history, low education, cardiovascular risk factors

Early symptoms

Episodic memory loss (recent events), disorientation to time, word-finding difficulty

Late symptoms

Personality change, behavioural disturbance, motor symptoms, incontinence

Investigations

MMSE/MoCA, bloods to exclude reversible causes, CT/MRI (atrophy), FDG-PET or CSF biomarkers

Treatment

Cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine); memantine for moderate-severe

Memantine

NMDA antagonist; for moderate-severe AD; monotherapy or add-on

Prognosis

Progressive; 3-10 years from diagnosis; common cause of death

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Delirium
topics.html:
Definition

Delirium (acute confusional state) is an acute, fluctuating disturbance of attention, awareness, and cognition, usually caused by an underlying medical condition, substance, or medication, with onset over hours to days.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria

Disturbance of attention, awareness, cognition; acute onset (hours-days); fluctuation; evidence of physiological cause

Prevalence in hospital

10-30% of medical inpatients; 50% in ICU; 50% post-hip fracture

Key features

Acute onset, fluctuating course, inattention, disorganised thinking, altered consciousness

Hyperactive vs hypoactive

Hyperactive: agitation, hallucinations; Hypoactive: withdrawn, sleepy (more common, often missed)

Causes (DIMES)

Drugs, Infection, Metabolic, Environmental/Emotional, Structural (stroke, bleed)

Predisposing factors

Dementia, advanced age, polypharmacy, sensory impairment, frailty

Investigations

Bloods (FBC, U&E, Ca, glucose, LFT, TSH, B12), sepsis screen, ECG, CXR, CT head if indicated

Treatment

Treat underlying cause; reorient; avoid restraints; antipsychotic if severely agitated

Antipsychotic choice

Haloperidol 0.5-1 mg or olanzapine; lowest dose, shortest time

Prognosis

Associated with increased mortality, prolonged hospital stay, increased dementia risk

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Frontotemporal Dementia And Other Dementias
topics.html:
Definition

Frontotemporal dementia (FTD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder primarily affecting the frontal and temporal lobes, with early personality, behaviour, or language changes (rather than memory), often with onset in middle age (45-65 years).

Core Clinical Content

FTD prevalence

5-10% of all dementia; common cause of young-onset dementia (<65)

FTD subtypes

Behavioural variant (bvFTD) and primary progressive aphasia (PPA)

Behavioural variant (bvFTD)

Early disinhibition, apathy, loss of empathy, compulsive behaviours, hyperorality

PPA subtypes

Non-fluent (grammar), semantic (word meaning), logopenic (word retrieval)

Pick disease

FTD with Pick bodies (tau-positive intraneuronal inclusions)

Pathology

Tau or TDP-43 inclusions; frontotemporal atrophy on imaging

Imaging

MRI: focal frontal and/or temporal atrophy; 'knife-edge' gyri

Genetics

30% familial; MAPT, GRN, C9ORF72 mutations

Treatment

No disease-modifying therapy; SSRI for behavioural symptoms; AVOID antipsychotics

Prognosis

Progressive; 6-11 years from diagnosis; younger onset often more rapid

topics.title: Vascular Dementia And Dementia With Lewy Bodies
topics.html:
Definition

Vascular dementia (VaD) is cognitive decline due to cerebrovascular disease (multi-infarct, strategic infarct, small vessel disease). Dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB) is a synucleinopathy with fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, and parkinsonism.

Core Clinical Content

Vascular dementia — second most common

15-25% of dementia; preventable via vascular risk factor control

VaD features

Stepwise decline, focal neurological signs, early executive dysfunction, gait disturbance

VaD risk factors

Hypertension, diabetes, smoking, hyperlipidaemia, atrial fibrillation

VaD prevention

BP control, antiplatelet, statin, lifestyle modifications

DLB prevalence

10-15% of dementia; male predominance

DLB core features

Fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, parkinsonism, REM sleep behaviour disorder

DLB supportive features

Severe neuroleptic sensitivity, autonomic dysfunction, hyposmia, depression

DLB vs Parkinson's dementia

DLB: dementia within 1 year of parkinsonism; PDD: parkinsonism precedes dementia by >1 year

DLB treatment

Cholinesterase inhibitors (rivastigmine); AVOID antipsychotics (severe sensitivity)

Prognosis

VaD: variable, stepwise; DLB: progressive, falls, fluctuating course

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Wernicke Korsakoff Syndrome And Alcohol Related Co
topics.html:
Definition

Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome comprises Wernicke's encephalopathy (acute, reversible with treatment) and Korsakoff syndrome (chronic amnestic syndrome, often irreversible), both due to thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency, commonly in alcohol use disorder.

Core Clinical Content

Wernicke's triad

Confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia (nystagmus, 6th nerve palsy)

Wernicke's mechanism

Thiamine deficiency; often precipitated by glucose infusion in deficient patients

Wernicke's treatment

Pabrinex IV (thiamine + pyridoxine) BEFORE glucose; high-dose

Korsakoff syndrome

Anterograde amnesia, confabulation, relatively preserved other cognition

Korsakoff prognosis

Often irreversible; only 20% recover significantly; confabulation may persist

Wernicke to Korsakoff

Untreated Wernicke's progresses to Korsakoff in 80%

Memory in Korsakoff

Anterograde amnesia (cannot form new memories); retrograde amnesia (variable); confabulation

Other alcohol-related cognitive disorders

Alcohol-related dementia (possibly reversible with abstinence)

Marchiafava-Bignami disease

Alcohol-related corpus callosum demyelination; rare

Thiamine-rich foods

Wholegrains, pork, beans, nuts, yeast extract

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

TopicKey Point
Wernicke's triadConfusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia (nystagmus, 6th nerve palsy)
Wernicke's mechanismThiamine deficiency; often precipitated by glucose infusion in deficient patient
Wernicke's treatmentPabrinex IV (thiamine + pyridoxine) BEFORE glucose; high-dose

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome and alcohol-related cognitive disorders?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome and alcohol-related cognitive disorders?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

topics.title: Anorexia Nervosa
topics.html:
Definition

Anorexia nervosa is an eating disorder characterised by restriction of energy intake, intense fear of gaining weight, and disturbance in self-perceived body weight/shape, with significantly low body weight. It often begins in adolescence and is more common in females.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria

Restriction of energy intake, intense fear of gaining weight, body image disturbance, significantly low weight

Types

Restricting type (no binge/purge) and binge-eating/purging type

Prevalence

0.5-1% of females; rare in males; onset 13-20 years

BMI categories (DSM-5)

Mild ≥17, moderate 16-16.99, severe 15-15.99, extreme <15

Physical signs

Lanugo, bradycardia, hypotension, hypothermia, amenorrhoea, osteoporosis

Investigations

FBC, U&E, Ca/Mg/Phos, LFTs, TFTs, ECG, DEXA for bone density

Hypokalaemia

Common (especially purging type); risk of cardiac arrhythmia

Refeeding syndrome

Hypophosphataemia, hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia, fluid overload with reintroduction of nutrition

Refeeding prevention

Start slowly (10-20 kcal/kg/day), supplement thiamine, monitor electrolytes

Treatment

Medical stabilisation + nutritional rehabilitation + psychotherapy (FBT for adolescents, CBT)

topics.title: Binge Eating Disorder And Arfid
topics.html:
Definition

Binge eating disorder (BED) is an eating disorder characterised by recurrent binge eating episodes WITHOUT compensatory behaviours, causing marked distress. ARFID (avoidant/restrictive food intake disorder) is avoidance of food without weight/shape concerns, often in children.

Core Clinical Content

BED criteria

Recurrent binge eating (≥1/week × 3 months) with distress, NO compensatory behaviours

BED prevalence

Most common eating disorder (~2-3%); affects all genders; associated with obesity

BED features

Eating rapidly, eating when not hungry, eating alone, feeling guilty after

BED vs bulimia

BED: no compensatory behaviours; bulimia: recurrent purging/fasting/exercise

BED treatment

CBT (first-line), interpersonal therapy; lisdexamfetamine (FDA approved)

ARFID

Avoidant/restrictive food intake disorder; food avoidance WITHOUT body image concerns

ARFID features

Sensory sensitivity, fear of choking/vomiting, lack of interest in eating

ARFID demographics

Children/adolescents; equal M:F; associated with autism, anxiety

ARFID complications

Weight loss, nutritional deficiencies, dependence on supplements

ARFID treatment

Family-based, CBT-AR, exposure therapy; medical/nutritional support

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Bulimia Nervosa
topics.html:
Definition

Bulimia nervosa is an eating disorder characterised by recurrent episodes of binge eating followed by compensatory behaviours (vomiting, laxatives, excessive exercise) to prevent weight gain, occurring at least weekly for 3 months, with self-evaluation unduly influenced by body shape/weight.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria

Recurrent binge eating + compensatory behaviours ≥1/week × 3 months; self-evaluation unduly influenced by body shape

Prevalence

1-2% of females; onset late teens/early 20s

Body weight

Usually normal or slightly above (unlike anorexia)

Binge eating

Eating large amount in discrete period (2 h) with sense of loss of control

Compensatory behaviours

Self-induced vomiting, laxatives, diuretics, fasting, excessive exercise

Physical signs

Dental enamel erosion, parotid enlargement, calluses on knuckles (Russell's sign), electrolyte disturbance

Hypokalaemia

Common due to vomiting/laxative abuse; risk of arrhythmia

Differential

Anorexia binge-purge type, binge eating disorder, MDD

Treatment

CBT (first-line); SSRI (fluoxetine 60 mg); no medication licensed specifically

Prognosis

Better than anorexia; 70% recover with treatment

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Bulimia nervosa?

Key features include: dsm-5 criteria, prevalence, body weight.

Q3. How is Bulimia nervosa diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Bulimia nervosa?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Bulimia nervosa?

topics.title: Ethics Confidentiality And Compulsory Treatment
topics.html:
Definition

Medical ethics in psychiatry involves the four principles of biomedical ethics (autonomy, beneficence, non-maleficence, justice) applied to mental health contexts, including confidentiality, consent, duty to warn, and decisions about compulsory treatment.

Core Clinical Content

Four principles (Beauchamp & Childress)

Autonomy, Beneficence, Non-maleficence, Justice

Autonomy

Patient's right to self-determination; requires capacity and informed consent

Beneficence

Acting in patient's best interest

Non-maleficence

First do no harm; avoid causing harm

Justice

Fair distribution of resources; treat similar patients similarly

Confidentiality

Default; can be broken in specific circumstances (risk to self/others, public interest)

Duty to warn (Tarasoff)

If patient poses serious threat to identifiable person, clinician has duty to protect (warn)

GMC confidentiality guidance

Disclose only if necessary, lawful, proportionate; inform patient first if possible

Compulsory treatment ethics

Justified when patient lacks capacity or poses serious risk; less restrictive options first

Capacity-based vs risk-based

UK: Mental Health Act (risk-based) vs Mental Capacity Act (capacity-based)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Mental Capacity Act And Consent
topics.html:
Definition

The Mental Capacity Act (MCA) 2005 provides a legal framework for decision-making for people aged ≥16 who lack capacity to make specific decisions. It is based on 5 principles and supports best-interests decision-making.

Core Clinical Content

MCA 5 principles

Presume capacity; support to make decisions; unwise decisions; best interests; least restrictive option

Two-stage capacity test

1) Diagnostic — does impairment of mind/brain exist? 2) Functional — does impairment affect this decision?

Four functional criteria

Understand, Retain, Weigh, Communicate (decision at this time)

Lasting Power of Attorney (LPA)

Property/financial LPA + Health/welfare LPA; must be registered

Court of Protection

Makes decisions for those without capacity; appoints Deputies

Independent Mental Capacity Advocate (IMCA)

For serious medical decisions, accommodation changes when no family/friends

Advance Decision (ADRT)

Refusal of treatment in advance; binding if valid and applicable

Deprivation of Liberty Safeguards (DoLS)

Protect those who lack capacity but are deprived of liberty (care homes, hospitals)

Liberty Protection Safeguards (LPS)

Replacing DoLS; new framework under MCA 2019 amendments

Best interests decision

Multi-disciplinary; considers past/present wishes, beliefs, feelings; less restrictive option

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Q1. What is the definition of Mental Capacity Act and consent?

The Mental Capacity Act (MCA) 2005 provides a legal framework for decision-making for people aged ≥16 who lack capacity to make specific decisions. It is based on 5 principles and supports best-interests decision-making.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Mental Capacity Act and consent?

topics.title: Mental Health Act Mha And Compulsory Detention
topics.html:
Definition

The Mental Health Act (MHA) 1983 (amended 2007) provides the legal framework for the assessment, treatment, and detention of people with mental disorders in England and Wales. It allows compulsory treatment for patients who lack capacity or pose a risk to themselves or others.

Core Clinical Content

MHA criteria for detention

Mental disorder of nature/degree warranting detention; appropriate treatment available; risk to self/others

Section 2 (MHA)

Admission for assessment; up to 28 days; AMHP + 2 doctors (one Section 12 approved)

Section 3 (MHA)

Admission for treatment; up to 6 months (renewable)

Section 4 (MHA)

Emergency admission; up to 72 hours; 1 doctor + AMHP (when Section 12 doctor not available)

Section 5(2) (MHA)

Doctor's holding power; up to 72 hours for already admitted patients

Section 135 (MHA)

Warrant to enter premises and remove person to place of safety

Section 136 (MHA)

Police power to remove person from public place to place of safety; up to 24 h

Nearest Relative

Defined by Act; can be displaced by court; has specific rights (request assessment, discharge)

Mental Health Act Tribunals

Patients detained under Section 2/3 can apply for tribunal hearing

Consent to treatment (Part IV)

Section 57 (ECT, neurosurgery) requires patient + SOAD consent; Section 58 (3 months) for detained patients

topics.title: Conversion Disorder Functional Neurological Sympto
topics.html:
Definition

Conversion disorder (functional neurological symptom disorder) is characterised by one or more symptoms of altered voluntary motor or sensory function incompatible with recognised neurological/medical conditions, not better explained by another disorder, causing significant distress or impairment.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria

≥1 symptom of altered voluntary motor/sensory function; clinical findings provide evidence of incompatibility; symptom not better explained by another disorder; causes distress/impairment

Common presentations

Weakness, paralysis, tremor, dystonia, gait disorder, seizures (PNES), blindness, deafness, anaesthesia

La belle indifférence

Lack of concern about symptoms (NOT specific or required for diagnosis)

Prevalence

~5 per 100,000; more common in women; onset in young adulthood

Positive signs

Hoover's sign (hip extension weakness), tremor entrainment, give-way weakness

Hoover's sign

Hip extension weakness corrects with contralateral hip flexion against resistance

PNES (psychogenic non-epileptic seizures)

Resemble epilepsy but no EEG changes; prolonged, eyes closed, waxing/waning

Risk factors

Recent stress, prior trauma, childhood abuse, female sex

Treatment

Clear positive diagnosis, physiotherapy, CBT, address comorbidities

Prognosis

Initial improvement in 50%; long-term symptoms persist in many

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

topics.title: Dissociative Disorders And Depersonalisation
topics.html:
Definition

Dissociative disorders are a group of conditions involving disruption of consciousness, memory, identity, emotion, perception, body representation, motor control, or behaviour. Common presentations include dissociative amnesia, fugue, depersonalisation/derealisation, and DID.

Core Clinical Content

Dissociative disorders (F44)

Dissociative amnesia, fugue, stupor, trance, motor, convulsions, anaesthesia

Dissociative amnesia

Inability to recall important autobiographical information; not due to medical condition

Dissociative fugue

Dissociative amnesia + purposeful travel; new identity may be assumed

Depersonalisation/derealisation

Feeling of unreality of self (depersonalisation) or surroundings (derealisation)

DPD (depersonalisation disorder)

Persistent or recurrent; significant distress; not due to substance/medical

Dissociative identity disorder (DID)

Formerly multiple personality disorder; ≥2 distinct personality states

DID controversy

Disputed validity; some consider iatrogenic; high comorbidity with childhood trauma

Dissociation association

Often with childhood trauma, borderline personality, PTSD

Differential

PTSD, complex PTSD, factitious, malingering, neurological (TLE, post-ictal)

Treatment

Trauma-focused therapy; phase-oriented (stabilisation, processing, integration); DBT skills

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Dissociative disorders are a group of conditions involving disruption of consciousness, memory, identity, emotion, perception, body representation, motor control, or behaviour. Common presentations include dissociative amnesia, fugue, depersonalisation/derealisation, and DID.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Dissociative disorders and depersonalisation?

Key features include: dissociative disorders (f44), dissociative amnesia, dissociative fugue.

Q3. How is Dissociative disorders and depersonalisation diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Dissociative disorders and depersonalisation?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Dissociative disorders and depersonalisation?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

topics.title: Factitious Disorder And Malingering
topics.html:
Definition

Factitious disorder is the intentional production of symptoms to assume the sick role (no external gain). Malingering is the intentional production of symptoms for external gain (avoiding work, obtaining drugs, financial compensation). Both differ from somatic symptom disorder (unconscious).

Core Clinical Content

Factitious disorder criteria

Falsification of signs/symptoms, presents self as ill, deceptive behaviour, no external incentives

Munchausen syndrome

Severe chronic form of factitious disorder; wandering, dramatic stories, multiple scars

Malingering

Intentional production for external gain (work avoidance, drugs, money)

Malingering — DSM-5

Not a psychiatric diagnosis; listed as 'Other conditions for clinical focus'

Distinguish from SSD

SSD: unconscious; factitious: conscious (no external gain); malingering: conscious (external gain)

Factitious disorder imposed on another (formerly Munchausen by proxy)

Caregiver produces illness in child; child protection issue

Management

Direct but non-confrontational discussion; avoid unnecessary investigations; address underlying needs

Prognosis

Poor; chronic, recurrent; difficult to treat

Legal aspects

Factitious disorder imposed on another = criminal offence; mandatory reporting

Sick role

Primary motivation in factitious disorder (no external gain)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Illness Anxiety Disorder
topics.html:
Definition

Illness anxiety disorder (formerly hypochondriasis) is characterised by preoccupation with having or acquiring a serious illness, with high health anxiety, despite minimal or no somatic symptoms. Patients have excessive health-related behaviours (seeking reassurance, checking body) or maladaptive avoidance.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria

Preoccupation with having/acquiring serious illness; somatic symptoms not present or mild; high anxiety; excessive health-related behaviours; ≥6 months

Distinguish from SSD

IAD: minimal somatic symptoms but high anxiety; SSD: real distressing symptoms + excessive reaction

Distinguish from OCD

IAD: focus on having illness; OCD: focus on contamination/harm regardless of health

Distinguish from somatic delusion

IAD: recognise fear as excessive (insight preserved); somatic delusion: fixed, unshakeable belief

Prevalence

1-2% of general population

Comorbidity

Anxiety, depression, OCD, personality disorders

Behaviour patterns

Reassurance seeking OR avoidance (cancel appointments, avoid news)

Treatment

CBT (first-line); SSRI; minimise investigations/reassurance-seeking

Reassurance trap

Brief reassurance helps; extensive reassurance-seeking reinforces anxiety

Prognosis

Chronic, fluctuating; can improve with treatment

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Illness anxiety disorder?

Key features include: dsm-5 criteria, distinguish from ssd, distinguish from ocd.

Q3. How is Illness anxiety disorder diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Illness anxiety disorder?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Illness anxiety disorder?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

topics.title: Somatic Symptom Disorder
topics.html:
Definition

Somatic symptom disorder (formerly somatisation disorder / somatoform disorder) is characterised by one or more distressing somatic symptoms with excessive thoughts, feelings, or behaviours related to the symptoms, persisting >6 months.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria

≥1 distressing somatic symptom; excessive thoughts/feelings/behaviours; ≥6 months

Prevalence

5-7% of general population; more common in women; often presents in primary care

Common symptoms

Pain, fatigue, GI, neurological, sexual — often multiple

Key feature

Distress disproportionate to objective findings; NOT a 'malingered' condition

Distinguish from factitious/malingering

SSD: genuine symptoms, not consciously produced; factitious: conscious production; malingering: conscious production for external gain

Comorbidity

Depression, anxiety, other medical conditions (sometimes)

Management principles

Validate symptoms; regular short appointments; avoid unnecessary investigations

Treatment

CBT (first-line); SSRI for comorbid depression; address health anxiety

Prognosis

Chronic, fluctuating; early treatment improves outcome

Avoid

Dismissing as 'all in the head'; excessive investigations reinforce illness behaviour

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Somatic symptom disorder (formerly somatisation disorder / somatoform disorder) is characterised by one or more distressing somatic symptoms with excessive thoughts, feelings, or behaviours related to the symptoms, persisting >6 months.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Somatic symptom disorder?

Key features include: dsm-5 criteria, prevalence, common symptoms.

Q3. How is Somatic symptom disorder diagnosed?

Based on DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria, clinical assessment, and appropriate investigations.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Somatic symptom disorder?

Combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions per evidence-based guidelines.

Q5. What is the prognosis of Somatic symptom disorder?

Variable; depends on early intervention, treatment adherence, comorbidities.

topics.title: Bipolar Disorder Type I And Ii
topics.html:
Definition

Bipolar disorder is a recurrent mood disorder characterised by episodes of mania (type I) or hypomania (type II) alternating with depressive episodes. The lifetime prevalence is ~2-3%.

Core Clinical Content

Bipolar I

Mania (≥1 week, hospitalisation may be needed) + depressive episodes

Bipolar II

Hypomania (≥4 days) + major depression; never full mania

Mania symptoms (DIG FAST)

Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity increase, Sleep deficit, Talkativeness

Mania duration

≥1 week (or any duration if hospitalisation needed)

Hypomania

Similar to mania but <1 week, no marked functional impairment, no psychosis

Mixed features

Mania + depression simultaneously; high suicide risk

First-line for acute mania

Lithium, valproate, or atypical antipsychotic (olanzapine, risperidone, quetiapine)

Long-term prophylaxis

Lithium (gold standard, also ↓ suicide), valproate, lamotrigine

Lithium monitoring

TFTs (hypothyroid), U&E (renal), Ca (hyperparathyroidism), levels every 3-6 months

Antidepressant caution

Can trigger mania; use with mood stabiliser in bipolar

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Bipolar IIHypomania (≥4 days) + major depression; never full mania

Antidepressant cautionCan trigger mania; use with mood stabiliser in bipolar

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Bipolar disorder type I and II?

Bipolar disorder is a recurrent mood disorder characterised by episodes of mania (type I) or hypomania (type II) alternating with depressive episodes. The lifetime prevalence is ~2-3%.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Bipolar disorder type I and II?

Key features include: bipolar i, bipolar ii, mania symptoms (dig fast). These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Bipolar disorder type I and II diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Bipolar disorder type I and II?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Bipolar disorder type I and II?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Bipolar disorder type I and II — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

topics.title: Depression In Medical Illness
topics.html:
Definition

Depression comorbid with medical illness is common, often under-recognised, and worsens medical outcomes. It occurs in 20-40% of patients with chronic medical conditions and requires active screening and treatment.

Core Clinical Content

Prevalence in medical illness

20-40% in chronic conditions (cancer, stroke, MI, diabetes)

Post-stroke depression

~30-40%; peaks 3-6 months post-stroke; often missed

Post-MI depression

~20%; increases mortality and reinfarction risk

Cancer-related depression

Common in pancreatic, lung, breast; requires active screening

Parkinson's disease

Depression in 40-50%; may precede motor symptoms

Hypothyroidism

Common cause of depressive symptoms; check TFTs

Diagnosis in medical illness

Same criteria; somatic symptoms less specific in medical patients

Treatment

SSRI first-line; mirtazapine useful with insomnia/appetite loss

Drug interactions

SSRIs + tramadol (serotonin syndrome), SSRIs + warfarin (bleeding)

Bereavement-related depression

Distinguish normal grief from MDD; treat if severe/prolonged

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Major Depressive Disorder
topics.html:
Definition

Major depressive disorder (MDD) is a mood disorder characterised by persistent low mood and/or anhedonia for at least 2 weeks, with associated cognitive, behavioural, and somatic symptoms causing significant functional impairment.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria (SIG E CAPS)

Sleep, Interest loss, Guilt, Energy loss, Concentration, Appetite change, Psychomotor changes, Suicidal ideation + depressed mood or anhedonia ≥2 weeks

At least 5 of 9 symptoms

Must include either depressed mood OR anhedonia

Duration

≥2 weeks (usually much longer)

Prevalence

Lifetime ~15-20%; 2x more common in women

Risk factors

Previous episode, family history, chronic illness, substance use, stress, loss

Suicide risk

~15% of severe MDD; highest in first month of treatment

First-line treatment

SSRI or SNRI + CBT or IPT (combination > monotherapy)

Duration of treatment

Continue for 6-12 months after remission (first episode); longer for recurrent

ECT

For severe, treatment-resistant, psychotic, or life-threatening depression

Antidepressant onset

2-4 weeks for full effect; assess at 4-6 weeks

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Persistent Depressive Disorder Dysthymia
topics.html:
Definition

Persistent depressive disorder (formerly dysthymia) is a chronic form of depression with depressed mood for most of the day, more days than not, for at least 2 years (1 year in children/adolescents).

Core Clinical Content

Duration

≥2 years (1 year in children/adolescents)

Symptoms

Same as MDD but fewer symptoms (≥2 of 6: poor appetite, sleep disturbance, low energy, low self-esteem, poor concentration, hopelessness)

Course

Chronic, often lifelong; less acute episodes than MDD

Prevalence

2-3% of adults; 1.5-3x more common in women

Comorbidity

High comorbidity with anxiety, substance use, personality disorders

Differential

MDD (episodic), cyclothymia, depression secondary to medical condition

Treatment

Same as MDD: SSRI/SNRI + psychotherapy; longer-term maintenance

Response rate

Lower than MDD; chronic course limits response

Risk of MDD

May develop superimposed MDD ('double depression')

Function

Significant functional impairment despite mild symptoms

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

SymptomsSame as MDD but fewer symptoms (≥2 of 6: poor appetite, sleep disturbance, low e

topics.title: Postpartum Depression And Puerperal Psychosis
topics.html:
Definition

Postpartum depression is a major depressive episode occurring within 4 weeks (DSM-5) or up to 12 months of delivery. Puerperal psychosis is a severe postpartum psychiatric emergency with mania, depression, and psychosis, typically presenting within 2 weeks of delivery.

Core Clinical Content

Baby blues

Mild mood lability, tearful, 3-5 days postpartum, resolves by 10-14 days

Postpartum depression

Major depression within 4 weeks (DSM-5) or up to 12 months; 10-15% of mothers

Risk factors

Previous postpartum depression, family history, perinatal complications, lack of support

Puerperal psychosis

Psychiatric emergency; onset within 2 weeks; risk of infanticide

Puerperal psychosis features

Mania, depression, psychosis, disorganised behaviour, delirium-like features

Treatment of postpartum depression

SSRI (sertraline, sertraline safe in breastfeeding); CBT; ECT for severe

Treatment of puerperal psychosis

Urgent admission, antipsychotic, mood stabiliser; mother-baby unit if available

Breastfeeding considerations

Sertraline preferred (low levels in breast milk); avoid lithium, benzodiazepines

Suicide risk in postpartum

Suicide is a leading cause of maternal death in first year

Infanticide risk

Puerperal psychosis with command hallucinations or severe depression; urgent assessment

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Antisocial And Other Cluster B Personality Disorde
topics.html:
Definition

Antisocial personality disorder (ASPD) is a Cluster B personality disorder characterised by disregard for and violation of the rights of others, occurring after age 18, with evidence of conduct disorder before age 15.

Core Clinical Content

ASPD criteria

≥3 of 15: failure to conform to laws, deceitfulness, impulsivity, irritability, recklessness, irresponsibility, lack of remorse

Age requirement

≥18 years; evidence of conduct disorder <15 years

Prevalence

3% men, 1% women; higher in prisons

Conduct disorder

Repetitive violation of others' rights/age-appropriate norms in <18 years

Comorbidity

Substance use (80%), mood/anxiety disorders, ADHD, other PDs

Treatment

Difficult to treat; poor engagement; no effective pharmacotherapy

Therapy approaches

CBT, schema therapy, therapeutic community (e.g., Henderson Hospital)

Borderline vs Antisocial

BPD: self-harm, fear of abandonment; ASPD: aggression to others, lack of remorse

Other Cluster B

Histrionic (attention-seeking, dramatic), Narcissistic (grandiosity, lack of empathy)

Prognosis

Improves with age (40+); criminal behaviour often decreases; substance use continues

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Borderline Personality Disorder Bpd
topics.html:
Definition

Borderline personality disorder (BPD) is a Cluster B personality disorder characterised by pervasive instability in mood, interpersonal relationships, self-image, and behaviour, with marked impulsivity beginning in early adulthood.

Core Clinical Content

DSM-5 criteria

≥5 of 9: frantic efforts to avoid abandonment, unstable relationships, identity disturbance, impulsivity, suicidal behaviour, affective instability, chronic emptiness, inappropriate anger, dissociative/paranoid ideation

Prevalence

~1-2% of population; 75% female

Comorbidity

Depression, substance use, eating disorders, PTSD, other PDs

Suicide risk

10x general population; ~8% die by suicide

Dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT)

First-line; combines individual + group therapy, phone coaching

DBT components

Mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, interpersonal effectiveness

Other therapies

Mentalisation-based therapy (MBT), schema therapy, transference-focused therapy (TFP)

Pharmacotherapy

No licensed medication; treat comorbidities; mood stabilisers for impulsivity

Hospitalisation

Reserved for imminent risk of self-harm; brief; avoid reinforcement of maladaptive patterns

Prognosis

Improves with age; 50% remit by 10 years; good response to DBT

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Borderline personality disorder (BPD)?

Borderline personality disorder (BPD) is a Cluster B personality disorder characterised by pervasive instability in mood, interpersonal relationships, self-image, and behaviour, with marked impulsivity beginning in early adulthood.

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Borderline personality disorder (BPD)?

Key features include: dsm-5 criteria, prevalence, comorbidity. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Borderline personality disorder (BPD) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Borderline personality disorder (BPD)?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Borderline personality disorder (BPD)?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Borderline personality disorder (BPD) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)

topics.title: Cluster A Personality Disorders Paranoid Schizoid
topics.html:
Definition

Cluster A personality disorders (paranoid, schizoid, schizotypal) are characterised by odd, eccentric, or paranoid thinking and behaviour. They may share features with schizophrenia spectrum disorders but are typically egosyntonic and less severe.

Core Clinical Content

Cluster A

Odd/eccentric; 'mad' (similar to schizophrenia)

Paranoid PD

Pervasive distrust, suspiciousness, jealousy; reluctant to confide; bears grudges

Paranoid PD vs delusional disorder

PD: pervasive pattern (not fixed delusion); DD: non-bizarre delusion, fixed

Schizoid PD

Detachment from social relationships; restricted emotional expression; indifference to praise/criticism

Schizoid vs avoidant

Schizoid: indifferent to relationships; avoidant: wants but fears relationships

Schizotypal PD

Acute discomfort with close relationships; cognitive/perceptual distortions; eccentric behaviour

Schizotypal vs schizophrenia

Schizotypal: brief/transient psychotic symptoms, reality testing preserved, egosyntonic; schizophrenia: frank psychosis

Cluster A vs schizophrenia

Cluster A: subtle, chronic, ego-syntonic; schizophrenia: frank psychosis, deterioration

Treatment

Difficult to treat; low engagement; support; cautious use of antipsychotics for transient psychosis

Prognosis

Stable personality traits; some risk of progression to schizophrenia (esp. schizotypal)

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

topics.title: Cluster C Personality Disorders Avoidant Dependent
topics.html:
Definition

Cluster C personality disorders (avoidant, dependent, obsessive-compulsive) are characterised by anxiety, fear, and avoidance. They are the most common personality disorders and often present in general medical settings.

Core Clinical Content

Cluster C

Anxious/fearful; 'worried/wet'

Avoidant PD

Social inhibition, feelings of inadequacy, hypersensitivity to criticism; wants but fears relationships

Avoidant vs schizoid

Avoidant: wants relationships; schizoid: doesn't want them

Avoidant vs social anxiety

PD: pervasive, ego-syntonic lifelong; SAD: episodic, ego-dystonic

Dependent PD

Pervasive need to be taken care of; submissive, clinging; fear of separation

Dependent PD risk

Higher risk of depression, abuse, somatic symptoms

Dependent vs dependent traits

PD: pervasive, rigid, maladaptive; traits: situational

OCPD

Preoccupation with order, perfectionism, control; ego-syntonic

OCPD vs OCD

OCPD: personality (egosyntonic); OCD: ego-dystonic obsessions/compulsions; often comorbid

Cluster C treatment

Psychotherapy (CBT, schema therapy, supportive); SSRIs for anxiety/depression; longer-term

topics.title: Personality Disorders Overview Clusters A B C
topics.html:
Definition

Personality disorders are enduring, inflexible, maladaptive patterns of inner experience and behaviour that deviate from cultural expectations, causing distress or impairment. They are classified into three clusters: A (odd/eccentric), B (dramatic/erratic), and C (anxious/fearful).

Core Clinical Content

Cluster A — odd/eccentric

Paranoid, Schizoid, Schizotypal

Cluster B — dramatic/erratic

Antisocial, Borderline, Histrionic, Narcissistic

Cluster C — anxious/fearful

Avoidant, Dependent, Obsessive-compulsive

General criteria

Enduring pattern, deviates from cultural norms, pervasive, inflexibility, onset by adolescence/adulthood

Prevalence

10-13% of general population; cluster C most common

Comorbidity

High with mood, anxiety, substance use, eating disorders

Treatment

Long-term psychotherapy (DBT for BPD, mentalisation-based, schema therapy); medication for comorbidities

Prognosis

Tends to improve with age; some evidence of neuroplasticity

Personality change

Distinguish from personality disorder (temporary) vs PD (enduring)

Cluster A vs schizophrenia

PD: eccentric but reality testing preserved; schizophrenia: frank psychosis

FCPS/MRCP High-Yield Summary

Viva Questions

Q1. What is the definition of Personality disorders overview (clusters A, B, C)?

Personality disorders are enduring, inflexible, maladaptive patterns of inner experience and behaviour that deviate from cultural expectations, causing distress or impairment. They are classified into three clusters: A (odd/eccentric), B (dramatic/erratic), and C (anxious/fearful).

Q2. What are the key clinical features of Personality disorders overview (clusters A, B, C)?

Key features include: cluster a — odd/eccentric, cluster b — dramatic/erratic, cluster c — anxious/fearful. These are essential for FCPS/MRCP exam recognition.

Q3. How is Personality disorders overview (clusters A, B, C) diagnosed?

Diagnosis is based on clinical features per DSM-5/ICD-11 criteria. Key diagnostic considerations are detailed in the Core Clinical Content section above.

Q4. What is the first-line treatment for Personality disorders overview (clusters A, B, C)?

First-line management typically includes a combination of pharmacological and psychological interventions, tailored to severity and patient preference. See Core Clinical Content for specific recommendations.

Q5. What are the prognosis and complications of Personality disorders overview (clusters A, B, C)?

Prognosis depends on early recognition, adherence to treatment, and management of comorbidities. See specific complications in Core Clinical Content.

Confusions & Mnemonics

  • Mnemonic for Personality disorders overview (clusters A, B, C) — see key points in Core Clinical Content
  • 'SIG E CAPS' for depression criteria: Sleep, Interest, Guilt, Energy, Concentration, Appetite, Psychomotor, Suicidal
  • 'DIG FAST' for mania: Distractibility, Indiscretion, Grandiosity, Flight of ideas, Activity, Sleep, Talkativeness
  • 'ABCDEFGHI' for MSE: Appearance, Behaviour, Speech, Mood, Affect, Thought form, Thought content, Perception, Cognition, Insight (with Risk)
  • 'First-rank (Schneider)' for schizophrenia — 'I am the agent of all my actions' (thoughts/acts not under own control = passivity phenomena)
